# Monolith → Microservices: Decision Framework

## First principles

Microservices are a distributed-systems tax paid in exchange for independent deployability and independent scalability — network calls replacing function calls, distributed data consistency instead of ACID transactions, N times the operational surface area, and service boundaries that lock in org design. If nothing you have requires independent deployability or independent scalability, you're paying that tax for nothing. So the real question isn't "monolith vs. microservices" — it's "what specific constraint is the monolith imposing on us, and is full decomposition the cheapest fix for that constraint." Treat a modular monolith (enforced module boundaries, internal APIs, per-module ownership and CI, feature flags) as the null hypothesis you must affirmatively reject before greenlighting an 18-month rewrite — it captures most of the organizational benefit of microservices at a fraction of the cost, and every case where it fails to solve the actual problem should be nameable in specific terms, not asserted in the abstract.

## Decision framework

Work through these gates in order. Failing any one of them is a reason to stop short of a full rebuild.

**Gate 1 — Diagnose the actual bottleneck.** List the concrete symptoms driving the proposal (deploy freezes, teams merge-conflicting, a scaling hotspot in one subsystem, incident blast radius, onboarding time). Most of these have cheaper fixes than a rewrite:
- Team-coordination bottleneck (too many people on one deploy pipeline) → a Conway's Law problem, addressable with module boundaries and separate CI/ownership before it requires network boundaries.
- Genuine differential scaling (one subsystem needs 50x the compute of the rest) → extract *that one subsystem*, not the whole system.
- Coupling/spaghetti-dependency bottleneck → modularize first; this alone de-risks any later extraction and is commonly 10x cheaper.
- Reliability/blast-radius bottleneck → often addressable with process and fault isolation inside a modular monolith.
- Slow CI, no feature flags, thin test coverage, unclear code ownership → these are process problems that microservices will not fix and will make harder to fix, because you'll now have N pipelines with the same bad practices.

**Gate 2 — Check org topology against Conway's Law.** You need roughly one autonomous, full-stack team (4–8 engineers, with product, engineering, and on-call ownership) per service cluster you intend to create. Below roughly 20–30 engineers total, you don't have enough teams to staff both feature work and a platform function — microservices will just distribute one team's cognitive load across a network. The classic failure pattern is 20 engineers creating 40 microservices: more deployable units than there are people to operate them.

**Gate 3 — Verify the operational substrate exists or is explicitly budgeted.** Microservices assume: CI/CD automation per service, distributed tracing and centralized logging (not optional — debugging a request across a dozen services without it is unworkable), a deployment platform (Kubernetes or equivalent), service discovery, an API gateway, contract testing and versioning discipline, a data-consistency strategy (sagas, outbox pattern), on-call ownership per service, and a dedicated platform/infra function of roughly 2–4 people who aren't also carrying a feature roadmap. If most of this doesn't exist today, the 18-month clock is really "6–9 months building platform infrastructure, then migrate" — budget that honestly instead of hiding it inside "migration."

**Gate 4 — Confirm the domain model is stable enough to draw boundaries.** Service boundaries are bets on what will and won't change together. If the core data model is still churning quarterly — pre-PMF in a segment, a recent pivot, active M&A integration — you will draw the wrong boundaries and pay to redraw them later, which is the single most underestimated cost in any decomposition. Rule of thumb: don't decompose a domain area until it's been stable for 2+ quarters.

**Gate 5 — Cost it in real dollars and time, against the cheap alternative, explicitly.** Put "modularize the monolith + extract the 1–2 genuinely hot subsystems" next to "full microservices rewrite" and force real numbers: engineering-months, a platform team for the duration, and an expected feature-velocity dip (commonly cited in the 20–40% range for 6–12 months of heavy migration, sometimes longer). For an 18-month effort at a genuinely mid-size company, fully loaded cost routinely lands in the low-to-high seven figures once platform headcount and infra spend are counted, not just the feature engineers' time. If leadership hasn't seen and accepted that number next to the cheaper alternative's number, the decision isn't real yet — it's aesthetic.

**Decision rule:** proceed with full decomposition only if (a) the pain is concentrated on specific, named subsystems rather than uniform or process-driven; (b) org topology already has 4+ teams that want independent release cadences; (c) the platform substrate is funded and staffed before service #1 ships; (d) the domain model has been stable for 2+ quarters; and (e) leadership has explicitly accepted the dollar cost and the velocity dip. If all five hold, migrate incrementally with the strangler fig pattern — a facade/gateway in front of the monolith, one service peeled off at a time, both systems shipping features concurrently — never as a big-bang rewrite. If leadership won't fund the monolith team and the migration team simultaneously, don't start.

For a genuinely mid-size SaaS (roughly 20–60 engineers, one primary product surface), the base rate should favor "modular monolith + targeted extraction of 1–3 genuinely hot subsystems" over a full rewrite. Full decomposition is right only when you can name the specific subsystems with a differential-scaling or team-ownership problem the monolith can't solve any other way — not as a wholesale architecture bet.

## Migration failure modes

In roughly descending order of how often they actually derail these projects:

1. **Big-bang rewrite / frozen roadmap.** Treating 18 months as a ship date rather than a budget, and freezing monolith feature work to "finish the migration," is close to a guaranteed failure — stakeholders revolt around month 6, or the org runs two codebases and the monolith keeps evolving underneath the migration, so the target never stops moving. Correct pattern: strangler fig, incremental extraction, monolith kept shipping the whole time.

2. **Distributed monolith.** Boundaries drawn around technical layers or database tables rather than business capabilities mean services still deploy in lockstep — via shared libraries, a shared database, or synchronous call chains where A must call B must call C to answer one request. Tell: if you can't deploy service X without also deploying service Y in the same window, you've built this. It's worse than the monolith you started with: network latency and partial-failure modes on top of coupled deploys. Segment's widely cited 2018 postmortem is the canonical case study here — moving to one microservice per integration meant operational overhead (repos, queues, shared-library breakage) scaled linearly with every new integration added, and defect rates and velocity moved in the wrong direction until they consolidated back into a monolith.

3. **Shared database left undecomposed.** Splitting the code is roughly 20% of the effort; splitting the data is 80%. Shared tables, cross-boundary foreign keys, and transactions that used to be a single ACID commit become distributed sagas. Teams that plan the code split and treat data as an afterthought routinely blow the timeline by 2–3x, and unhandled edge cases (partial failure, retries, idempotency) produce silent data corruption — double charges, orphaned records — that surfaces months later and is expensive to trace back to the migration.

4. **Boundary mis-draws requiring re-decomposition.** Guessed or org-chart-driven boundaries instead of actual bounded contexts produce chatty, high-latency service graphs, discovered six months in when APIs are already public and consumed by other teams — expensive to redraw at that point.

5. **Observability arriving after the services do.** Teams build the services first and tracing/logging second. An incident that used to be "grep one log file" becomes "correlate a dozen services' logs by hand," so MTTR gets measurably *worse* during the transition — often the single biggest credibility hit the project takes internally.

6. **Latency and failure-mode regression.** In-process function calls become network calls. Without disciplined timeouts, retries, circuit breakers, and idempotency — usually being implemented for the first time by the team doing it — you introduce cascading-failure risk and p99 regressions that didn't exist in the monolith.

7. **Version skew and contract breakage.** Without contract testing and API versioning discipline from day one, service A ships a breaking change that silently breaks service B in production. What used to be a compile-time error in the monolith becomes a runtime incident.

8. **Operational cost surprise.** N services × (logging + monitoring + on-call + CI pipeline + deploy config) is a step-function increase in overhead. Without dedicated platform investment to absorb this per-service tax, SRE/infra toil scales linearly or worse with service count and quietly eats the productivity gains the migration was supposed to buy.

9. **Strangler fig stalls at 60–70%.** The easy, low-risk modules get extracted first; the highest-value, gnarliest core — billing, auth, the central domain model — never does, because it's too risky to touch under a deadline. The org ends up running two architectures indefinitely, permanently paying the operational cost of both.

10. **Sunk-cost momentum.** Once six-plus months and real budget are committed, organizations keep going past the point where the original justification stopped holding — a pivot changed the domain model, a reorg shrank the team below the threshold that justified decomposition — because stopping feels like admitting failure. This is a governance failure, not a technical one, and it's the most expensive item on this list precisely because nothing technical stops it; only an explicit, pre-committed kill-switch does.

## When it's the wrong call

- **Team size doesn't support it.** Below roughly 20–30 engineers, the coordination overhead of microservices reliably exceeds the coordination overhead of a well-modularized monolith. This is the most common reason mid-size SaaS companies regret the move — "mid-size" is frequently exactly the size where it fails.
- **The pain is uniform, not concentrated.** If deploy pain and merge conflicts are spread evenly across the codebase rather than concentrated in specific modules, that's a process/CI/testing problem microservices won't fix — it will multiply the same bad practices across N pipelines instead.
- **No dedicated, funded platform function.** If the same product engineers who'd build features are expected to also build and operate the service mesh, deploy infrastructure, and observability stack on top of their existing roadmap, the migration will starve one or both.
- **Scaling needs are uniform, not differential.** If all subsystems scale together (typical CRUD SaaS without a hot, differentially-scaling subsystem), there's no scaling justification — only an org-design one, and org problems don't require network boundaries.
- **The domain model is still volatile** — a recent pivot, pre-PMF in a segment, active M&A integration. Stabilize the domain inside the monolith first.
- **The actual bottleneck is the database, not the code.** If profiling shows a handful of expensive queries or an undersized DB tier, that's solved with read replicas, caching, or a bounded-context split at the data layer alone.
- **The business can't absorb the velocity dip.** An 18-month project typically costs 6–12 months of materially reduced feature output during peak migration. In a competitive land-grab phase, ahead of major enterprise renewals, or in an IPO/compliance-audit window, that bet is usually wrong — and the failure blast radius and the velocity dip both land at the worst possible time.
- **It's driven by resume-building or industry fashion** ("mature companies do microservices") rather than a named, measured bottleneck. This is extremely common and is, on its own, one of the most reliable predictors of failure.
- **A cheap fix already tested clean.** If a short modularization spike (enforced module boundaries, internal APIs, CI checks) resolves the measured pain, running the 18-month rewrite anyway is pure overengineering.

## What to measure

**Before deciding (diagnostic baseline, and the actual go/no-go gate):**
- The four DORA metrics — deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change-failure rate, MTTR — segmented *by subsystem/team*, not aggregated. This is what tells you whether pain is concentrated (a real decomposition signal) or uniform (a process signal).
- Cross-team PR/merge-conflict rate and % of files touched by more than two teams in the last quarter — actual coupling, not perceived coupling.
- CI pipeline duration and its trend over the last 12 months.
- Per-subsystem CPU/memory/traffic profile — do subsystems actually have divergent scaling needs, or is load roughly uniform?
- Team topology map vs. code-module map — do they already roughly align (good sign for extraction) or are they orthogonal (bad sign — extraction will be messy)?
- Incident postmortems for the last two quarters: what fraction actually trace back to coupling/blast-radius issues vs. code quality, process, or infra capacity?
- Current infra/on-call cost per engineer, as the baseline you'll compare against later.

**During migration (checked monthly or quarterly — this is the mechanism that defeats sunk-cost momentum, not just a status report):**
- Feature throughput vs. baseline. Expect a dip; the gate is whether it stays within the budgeted range and is recovering on schedule (by month 9–12), not whether it dipped at all.
- % of user-facing requests still requiring synchronous cross-service calls, and the count of services that cannot be deployed independently of another — both should trend toward zero. Flat or rising means you're building a distributed monolith.
- p50/p95/p99 latency and error rate for the top user-facing flows that now cross service boundaries.
- Incident count and MTTR attributable specifically to distributed-systems failure modes (timeouts, partial failures, retry storms) — expect a rise before a fall; watch the trend, not the level.
- % of the target domain migrated vs. % of the 18-month timeline/budget consumed — catches strangler-fig stall and schedule slip early.
- On-call load (pages per engineer per week) — rising without a corresponding rise in platform headcount means the org is accruing operational debt.
- Infra spend delta vs. plan.

**After (validate against the original problem, not against "did we finish the rewrite"):**
- Did deploy frequency, lead time, and change-failure rate actually improve for the teams that got independence, compared to the pre-migration baseline? This should be the primary success metric, since it's usually the actual justification from Gate 1.
- Did the specific, concentrated pain named at the outset actually resolve?
- Cost per unit of scale for the subsystems that motivated the split — did independent scaling reduce infra cost or improve latency there specifically?
- Total infra + platform + on-call cost per engineer vs. baseline — the number most likely to move in the wrong direction and get quietly ignored once the migration is declared "done."

The single most important discipline: define the 2–4 metrics that constitute success, with explicit thresholds and checkpoints (e.g., month 6 and month 12), *before* starting — and give someone with real authority the explicit mandate to kill or descope the migration if those thresholds aren't met, rather than letting sunk cost carry it to month 18 regardless of what the data says. If, a year in, deploy lead time and change-failure rate haven't moved and cost-per-engineer has risen, the migration failed on its own terms no matter how architecturally clean the new service boundaries look — that's the number to force the org to confront, not completion of the rewrite itself.

# Globally-Distributed Ad-Impression Billing Counter

## The reframing that makes this solvable

"1M increments/sec, survive a full region loss, never over- or under-count" is unsatisfiable if you read it literally as *one shared integer that everyone safely `+1`s*. A single linearizable counter is a single hot key, which means a single consensus-group leader that all writes serialize through. Two independent walls stop it at 1M/sec:

- **The hot-key wall.** One Raft/Paxos leader tops out around 10k–100k log appends/sec (CPU/fsync/NIC bound) no matter how you pipeline — already >10× short of 1M/sec.
- **The serialization wall.** If reads are linearizable and an increment is read-modify-write, each op waits a full commit round, so throughput collapses to ~`1/RTT`. At 50–150 ms inter-region RTT that's **~7–20 ops/sec** (single digits if you need two round trips). To reach 1M/sec that way you'd need ~66,000 shards *and* still pay WAN latency on every write — i.e., you'd have re-derived a sharded design, badly.

So sharding is forced by physics, not taste. But the moment you shard, "one linearizable number" is gone. The resolution is to stop counting *mutations* and start counting *facts*:

> **The count is `|distinct durably-recorded impression IDs|`, derived by set-union, not by racing `+1`s.**

Because retries are *mandatory* in any system that survives failures (a client that doesn't get an ack must resend), and retries are what cause double-counting, exactness is impossible unless each impression is an immutable, idempotently-keyed event. This is the published-and-proven pattern behind Google's ads-billing stack (Photon's cross-region dedup registry over a Paxos-replicated ID store; F1/Spanner built for AdWords). We follow it.

The second reframing: **there is no single number.** Three consumers have three different needs, backed by the *same* durable facts but read on different paths:

| Consumer | Needs | Guarantee it gets |
|---|---|---|
| Pacing / frequency capping | Low latency, regional view, slight staleness OK | AP, sub-second |
| Dashboards / analytics | Seconds-to-minutes lag OK | Strong Eventual Consistency |
| **Billing / invoicing** | **Exact, auditable, correct at period close** — *not* real-time | Exact, from replayed ledger |

Only billing carries the "never over/under-count" requirement, and it explicitly does **not** need to be real-time. Optimizing the exact path for correctness-at-close instead of correctness-at-every-instant is what makes every requirement individually achievable.

## Consistency model

Three layers, each with the weakest guarantee that still does its job:

**1. Idempotency gate — CP (linearizable), globally sharded.**
The only place we pay for consensus. A horizontally sharded, multi-region-replicated strongly-consistent store (Spanner / CockroachDB / FoundationDB class). Sharded by `hash(event_id) mod N` (N ≈ few thousand); **each shard is its own consensus group of 3–5 replicas across distinct regions/failure domains**, majority quorum. The operation is a single atomic **conditional insert — `INSERT event_id IF NOT EXISTS`** — that commits only when a cross-region majority acks. First insert of an ID wins and counts; every later arrival of that ID is a no-op. Dedup and durable commit are *the same atomic operation*, so there is no window between "checked" and "recorded" in which a crash could double-count or drop. Because sharding is by `hash(event_id)`, the *same* ID always routes to the *same* consensus group regardless of which region a retry lands in — so dedup stays authoritative across regional failover, which is exactly the scenario we must survive.

**2. Aggregate counter — AP / Strong Eventual Consistency, via G-Counter CRDT.**
Every successful (first-seen) insert bumps one grow-only register. The global figure is a **G-Counter**: a vector with one entry per shard, each shard the sole writer of its own entry; **value = sum of entries, merge = element-wise max**. Max-merge is commutative, associative, and *idempotent*, so replays, reordering, partial merges, or re-delivery from a recovering region can never lose or double a count — replicas that have seen the same commits converge to the identical value. No coordination is needed to read a global total; any region answers from its local replica. (Shipping deltas with a monotonic per-shard offset is an equivalent formulation; state-based max-merge is simpler to reason about.)

**3. Billing truth — the reconciled ledger, not the live counter.**
The invoiced number is computed by a windowed batch job that replays the deduplicated, immutable event log for a *closed* (watermarked) period and takes `COUNT(DISTINCT event_id)` per advertiser/campaign. The live CRDT counter is a best-effort operational artifact; the ledger is the source of truth. This is the CAP-honest split: the sliver that must never be wrong (dedup) is CP and pays latency; everything downstream is AP and cheap, because idempotent CRDT merges physically cannot corrupt an already-correct set of facts.

**The load-bearing invariant tying it together:** *the client is acked only after the event's idempotency record has committed to a cross-region majority quorum.* Not after local/AZ durability — after cross-region durability. This is what makes "acked ⇒ survives any single-region loss" true, and it is the one place teams get this wrong.

## Data path

```
Ad server wins auction, serves the ad to the user immediately
   │  (ad rendering is NOT blocked on anything below)
   ▼
[1] Mint event_id = hash(auction_id + creative_id + publisher_id + server_nonce)
      • server-generated, exactly once, at the winning ad server
      • client/browser beacons are unreliable (double-fire, replay) → carriers of the ID only, never sources
   ▼
[2] Append {event_id, campaign_id, event_time, region} to nearest regional durable log
      (Kafka/Kinesis-class), acks=all, min.insync.replicas≥2 across 3 AZs
      • absorbs the 1M/sec write spike with pure horizontal partitioning, no coordination
   ▼
[3] Stream processor (Flink-class, exactly-once checkpointing) consumes the log and issues the
    conditional insert into the sharded cross-region consensus store  ── THE IDEMPOTENCY GATE
      • commit = cross-region majority quorum
      • the client's billing-record ack is gated on THIS commit (client retries same event_id until it gets it)
   ▼
[4] First-seen insert increments that shard's G-Counter register (local, lock-free)
   ▼
[5] Regional aggregator sums local registers → near-real-time regional total (feeds pacing)
    Async cross-region gossip of per-shard registers every 1–5s → every region holds the full
    vector → sum = eventually-consistent global total (feeds dashboards)
   ▼
[6] Immutable audit log: every raw tuple compacted from Kafka to multi-region object storage,
    long retention (dispute resolution, "why was this charged" months later)
   ▼
[7] Billing finalization: windowed batch (watermarked) closes each period once no further retries
    are possible (bounded by max client retry TTL, e.g. 48h). COUNT(DISTINCT event_id) over the
    deduped ledger = the invoiced number. Events arriving after the watermark post as a correction
    to the NEXT period — a closed invoice is never silently reopened (standard ad-billing practice).
```

Two things worth making explicit, because they defuse the usual objections:

- **Serving latency ≠ billing-commit latency.** The impression is shown to the user with zero added latency. The cross-region quorum commit (tens–low-hundreds of ms) happens on the *billing record*, off the ad-rendering critical path. The ad server holds the event and retries in the background until it gets a cross-region-durable ack.
- **The consensus store is not the throughput bottleneck.** Kafka absorbs the 1M/sec burst; the gate does dedup at its own sharded pace. Synchronous replication adds *latency per commit, not a throughput ceiling*, because commits are pipelined (don't wait for batch N's ack before proposing N+1) and spread across thousands of independent shards.

*(Optional micro-optimization: a per-shard Bloom filter as a fast **negative** pre-check. A Bloom negative means "definitely new" → skip the store lookup on the overwhelmingly common not-a-duplicate path. A Bloom positive must always be verified against the authoritative store, since a false positive would drop a genuinely new event and undercount. Never treat the Bloom filter as truth.)*

## Failure handling

**AZ failure within a region.** Invisible to correctness. Intra-region sync replication (ISR/Raft) rides it out; at worst a brief leader-election blip.

**Full region failure — the headline requirement.** Two invariants, each simple and verifiable, cover it — the guarantee does *not* rest on the region surviving:
- *Committed data always has a surviving copy.* An acked event committed to a cross-region majority, so losing one region still leaves the data (and the shard keeps writing in degraded 2-of-3 mode; a standby region is added and caught up later). **Zero loss of acked data.**
- *Uncommitted data is always retried.* If an event hadn't reached quorum, the client never got an ack — so its own retry-on-timeout logic resends it, *with the same `event_id`*, to a healthy region. Same ID → same shard → the surviving replicas record it exactly once.

So under a full region loss you lose neither committed nor in-flight events; the exposure is a sub-second-to-low-seconds *coverage gap* during failover, not data loss.

**"Never over-count" is the strictly stronger guarantee here, by design.** Because every write is a conditional insert keyed on `event_id`, retries, region-recovery reconciliation, and a retry that briefly lands in two regions during rerouting all collapse to one. Over-counting is structurally impossible regardless of how messy the failure/retry sequence gets. We make it the stronger guarantee deliberately: over-charging an advertiser is a trust/legal/refund liability, whereas a bounded under-count is recoverable.

**Regional network partition (region alive but isolated).** Ingest keeps accepting and buffers to the local durable log (AP); the cross-region dedup commits for that region simply stall — CP means it produces *no answer*, never a *wrong* answer — and drain on reconnect. Other shards are unaffected.

**Single dedup shard loses quorum.** Only that key range is blocked (unavailable, not incorrect); upstream Kafka retains its data until the shard is writable again. A thin slice of keys, never a global outage.

**Region recovery.** Standard consensus catch-up from the surviving replicas. No special-case recovery code, because the mechanism is uniform in steady state and under failure — a property worth protecting, since bespoke recovery paths are where correctness bugs breed.

**The one honest limit.** If an impression's billing record was never durably committed *anywhere* **and** the originating ad server also dies before it can retry, that event is lost. This is not an engineering gap — it's the Two Generals' boundary: you cannot count a fact that was never reliably transmitted to any survivor. When this unavoidable sliver occurs, **we bias to under-count, never over-count** (revenue leakage is recoverable via SLA credits/estimation; a fabricated charge is a billing-integrity and legal problem). Every mechanism above — server-minted IDs, conditional-insert-only, quorum-before-ack — is chosen so failure modes fail toward "miss," not "duplicate."

**Backstop.** An hourly job reconciles `sum(live CRDT registers)` against the exact batch replay of the immutable ledger; drift beyond epsilon alerts. This catches *implementation bugs*, not the primary correctness mechanism — and no invoice is issued while a reconciliation mismatch is open.

## Trade-offs, each justified

- **Single global atomic counter — rejected.** Simple and obviously correct in isolation, but it's either one serialization point (can't hit 1M/sec, per the two walls above) or one failure domain (can't survive region loss). Sharding is mandatory, and sharding forbids single-number linearizability.
- **2PC / cross-region consensus per increment — rejected.** Correct but ~7–20 ops/sec per serialized key, and the coordinator is itself a failure point that must survive the very region loss we're designing for. We pay consensus only at the idempotency gate, sharded thousands of ways, so it's a *latency* cost per request, not a *throughput* ceiling.
- **Pure CRDT counters with no dedup gate — rejected.** Infinitely scalable and fully AP, but the retries that durability *requires* would double-count without upstream idempotency. This is the version that actually violates "never over-count."
- **Fire-and-forget local ack, replicate later — rejected.** Cheapest and fastest, but a region loss then takes the un-replicated tail *with no retry signal* (the client was already acked), turning under-count into an unbounded, silent risk instead of a self-healing one. This is why the ack must wait for cross-region quorum.
- **Client/browser-generated IDs — rejected.** Beacons double-fire and replay; trusting them as ID *sources* corrupts dedup. IDs are minted server-side, once; the beacon only carries them.
- **Store every event_id forever for live dedup — rejected.** Unbounded. The live gate TTLs its dedup state at the max plausible retry window (24–48h); the batch billing job does a second, *unbounded* DISTINCT pass over the immutable log, so the TTL is a cost optimization, never a correctness dependency.
- **Billing off the live counter — deliberately not done.** The live number is eventually consistent and can be transiently stale after a partition. Advertisers don't need sub-second billing accuracy; they need *correct daily/monthly totals*. Spending exactness to buy real-time-ness nobody asked for is a bad trade, so the invoiced number always comes from the replayed ledger.
- **Consensus-group placement — tunable.** 3 replicas in nearby regions (~10–20 ms RTT) minimize commit latency and survive any single-region loss; spreading replicas across continents (or going to 5 replicas) buys stronger fault-independence at higher latency. Pick per durability SLA.

## Capacity check

- 1M events/sec × ~300 B ≈ **300 MB/s** ingest, **86.4 B events/day**.
- ~4,000 dedup shards → **~250 commits/sec/shard** — trivial for a pipelined consensus group even across regions, and no hot shard because keys hash uniformly.
- Cross-region commit at ~100 ms ⇒ only **~100k events in flight** globally at any instant — negligible client-side buffering.

**Bottom line:** exactness comes from *idempotent conditional-insert + quorum-before-ack* at the write path (a bounded, per-shard problem that's cheap to make correct); scale comes from *massive sharding + G-Counter summation* (an aggregation problem that's cheap to make fast); the two are joined by treating the live global figure as an operationally useful approximation and the periodically-replayed immutable ledger as the actual billing ground truth. The only unrecoverable loss is the physically-impossible-to-avoid one, and it is deliberately biased toward under-counting.

# Globally-Distributed Exact Ad-Impression Counter

## 0. The reframing that makes this solvable

"1M increments/sec, survive a full region failure, never over- or under-count" is unsolvable if you think of it as *one mutable integer that many writers race to update*. A single linearizable register — Spanner row, etcd key, Redis `INCR` — forces every writer through one serialization point. Sharding that register fixes throughput but breaks the idea of "one true number," and a naive back-of-envelope (RTT-bound, no pipelining) makes cross-region synchronous replication look impossibly slow, which tempts people into pure fire-and-forget replication — which then silently loses or duplicates data under retries.

The move that resolves all of this at once: stop modeling impressions as *increments applied to a counter* and start modeling them as **immutable, uniquely-identified events**, where "the count" is `|deduplicated events successfully recorded|`. Once counting is redefined as **set cardinality of a durable, idempotently-keyed log** rather than *arithmetic on a shared mutable value*, retries, replays, reordering, and partial failures stop being correctness threats — a duplicate delivery of the same event is just a no-op. This is the same pattern production ad-serving systems actually use (Google's Photon/MillWheel-style exactly-once stream joining; Spanner's original production workload was an ads-billing backend for exactly this reason) — it isn't a novel trick, it's the standard answer to this exact problem.

The second move: **stop trying to make one number serve every reader.** Three different consumers need this count, with genuinely different consistency requirements:

| Consumer | Needs | Consistency |
|---|---|---|
| Budget pacing / frequency capping | Low latency, regional view, slight staleness tolerable (industry-standard soft caps with a buffer) | Fast, local, approximate |
| Dashboards / analytics | Global view, seconds-to-minutes lag fine | Eventually consistent |
| Billing / invoicing | Must be **exact**, but only needs to be correct **at period close**, not in real time | Exact, delayed |

Only the billing consumer has the hard "never over/under-count" requirement. Design for that one explicitly; the other two ride on the same durable data for free, at near-zero extra cost.

## 1. Consistency model

**Write/durability boundary — linearizable, CP, but geographically scoped.** Impressions are hash-partitioned across many shards. Each shard is a **synchronously-replicated, consensus-backed key-value range** (Raft/Paxos — e.g., a CockroachDB/FoundationDB/Spanner-style range, or a purpose-built Raft shard), with replicas placed across **a small "pod" of 2–3 geographically nearby regions** (same continent, ~10–20ms RTT), not scattered globally. A write is a single **conditional put** — `INSERT event_id IF NOT EXISTS` — and it is not acknowledged to the caller until it has **quorum-committed (majority of the pod)**. Dedup and durability are the *same atomic operation*, not two systems wired together — that's what closes off the classic check-then-write race and the "acked locally, lost before it reaches durable multi-region storage" gap that a two-hop design (fast local buffer → async promotion to a separate global store) would reopen. If you ack before the durability boundary you actually care about, you've quietly rebuilt the fire-and-forget design this whole exercise exists to avoid.

**Aggregation layer — Strong Eventual Consistency via CRDT.** Each shard exposes a monotonically-increasing local count (successful conditional-puts). The global total for any window is the sum of all shard counts — a **G-Counter** (grow-only CRDT): merge is commutative, associative, and idempotent, so out-of-order delivery, replays, or partial gossip between pods can never double-count or diverge, only converge. No coordination is needed to compute a global sum: any region can answer "current total" from whatever shard deltas it has locally received.

**Billing truth = reconciled batch replay, not the live counter.** The invoiced number is never read off the live CRDT sum. It's computed by a windowed batch job that closes each period only after a **watermark** confirms no more late arrivals are expected, then replays the deduplicated, immutable event log for that exact window. The live number is a best-effort operational artifact; the append-only log is the ledger of record.

This is a deliberate, CAP-theorem-honest split: the narrow slice that must never be wrong (the conditional-put dedup/durability gate) pays consensus latency and is CP; everything downstream — aggregation, dashboards, pacing — is AP/SEC and effectively free, because idempotent CRDT merges cannot corrupt a set of facts that's already correct.

## 2. Data path

```
Ad-serving decision (server-side, at auction/render time)
  → mint event_id ONCE, server-side (hash of auction_id+creative_id+publisher_id+nonce,
     or UUIDv7), embed it as a literal value in the tracking pixel/beacon URL
  → client/SDK only ever ECHOES that id back — never generates or recomputes it
       [critical: if the client computed its own id per beacon-fire, a client-side
        double-fire bug would mint two different ids for one real impression and
        dedup-by-id would silently miss it]
  → edge/ad-server buffers to LOCAL DISK (not memory) before any network call
  → micro-batches events by destination shard (batch ~1-10K events / ~5-10ms linger,
     the same batch.size+linger.ms pattern every Kafka producer already uses)
  → sends batch to nearest quorum-capable pod, pipelines many batches concurrently
     (don't block batch N+1 on batch N's ack)
  → shard = Raft/Paxos range replicated across a 3-region pod, 2-of-3 quorum
  → conditional put per event_id (create-if-absent) is the SAME operation as the
     durability commit
  → ack to edge ONLY after quorum commit  →  edge marks batch confirmed, drops from
     local disk buffer
  → on timeout/no-ack, edge retries the identical batch/ids to a different reachable
     pod — safe, because dedup collapses retries to one count regardless of which
     pod eventually processes it
  → committed shard log is continuously exported (changefeed/CDC) to immutable
     object storage (S3/GCS) — the audit/dispute-resolution ground truth
  → per-shard G-Counter deltas gossip asynchronously across pods (~1-5s target lag)
     → any region sums its local vector of shard counts → global live/pacing/
        dashboard total, no cross-region read required
  → at period close: watermarked batch job (Spark/Flink) replays the full
     deduplicated log for the closed window → this count, not the live counter,
     is what's invoiced. Events arriving after the watermark post as a correction
     to the NEXT period's ledger — never reopen a closed invoice.
  → reconciliation: batch replay count vs. live CRDT sum diffed continuously;
     mismatch beyond epsilon pages on-call (this catches bugs; it is not the
     primary correctness mechanism, which is the quorum+dedup gate itself)
```

**Why this clears 1M/sec despite synchronous cross-region commits.** Synchronous replication adds *latency per commit*, not a *ceiling on aggregate throughput*, as long as commits are pipelined — the same principle (Little's Law: concurrency = throughput × latency) that lets TCP and every production Raft/Paxos implementation sustain high throughput over long-RTT links. Concretely, at ~100ms pod-internal RTT: with **no batching at all**, ~100 concurrent in-flight commits per shard already sustains 1,000 events/sec/shard, so ~1,000 shards clears 1M/sec with comfortable headroom. With realistic client-side micro-batching (e.g., 1,000 events per commit, ~100 batches pipelined), a *single* shard can clear >1M events/sec in isolation — meaning shard count becomes a resilience/blast-radius dial, not a throughput necessity. A design that assumes each shard is stuck at "1 write per round-trip" (implying tens of thousands of shards) is solving the wrong problem; that ceiling only applies if you refuse to pipeline, which no real consensus implementation does. Pod-scoping (3 *nearby* regions, not a globally-spread quorum) is what keeps that per-commit RTT to ~10–20ms instead of ~150–300ms — it's also what keeps every write's success from being coupled to the health of a distant, unrelated region.

**Storage is trivial at this scale.** Bounding the live dedup index to a 24–48h TTL (the max plausible span of client-side disk-buffered retries) costs on the order of single-digit terabytes total (~7–14TB across the full key space at 1M/sec sustained, well under 5GB per shard at a few thousand shards) — the TTL bound is an optimization, not a correctness dependency, because the billing batch job does a second, *unbounded* dedup pass over the full closed window from the immutable log as the final backstop.

**Optional fast path:** a Bloom filter per shard can short-circuit the common "definitely new" case (Bloom filters have no false negatives, so "definitely not seen" is always trustworthy) to skip the exact-lookup cost — but it must never be used as the source of truth, since a false positive ("possibly seen") would silently cause an undercount if trusted directly.

## 3. Failure handling

- **AZ failure inside a region:** invisible to correctness — standard Raft/ISR leader re-election inside the pod; brief latency blip only.
- **Full failure of one region in a pod:** the surviving 2-of-3 still form quorum, so the shard keeps accepting writes uninterrupted in degraded (under-replicated) mode. Traffic destined for the dead region fails over via anycast/GSLB to a surviving pod member (or, if the whole local pod is affected, to the next-nearest pod, at slightly higher latency, temporarily). A standby replica is added and catches up once a healthy region is available. No special-case recovery path — steady-state and failure-recovery use the identical Raft catch-up mechanism.
- **Region isolated but alive (network partition, not a crash):** it can't reach its pod-mates, so it can't independently form quorum and must not accept writes as if it could (that would reintroduce the ack-before-true-durability bug) — clients reroute to a reachable quorum-capable pod member instead, exactly as in a full failure. The isolated region simply falls behind and catches up on reconnection.
- **The zero-loss argument, stated as an invariant, not a hope:** at the instant of any failure, every in-flight event is in exactly one of two states. (a) It quorum-committed — by definition ≥2 regions hold it, so losing 1 region loses zero committed data. (b) It did not quorum-commit — by definition the edge never received an ack, so the edge's own retry logic (disk-backed, same `event_id`) resends it to a different reachable pod, where dedup collapses the retry to exactly one count. "Never under-count" therefore doesn't depend on any specific region surviving; it depends only on (a) committed data always having a surviving quorum copy and (b) an edge that never got an ack always retrying. "Never over-count" is structurally guaranteed independent of failure timing, because every retry — however many pods it eventually touches — is gated by the same conditional-put-on-`event_id`.
- **The one genuinely irreducible edge case:** if the edge device itself is destroyed *and* every region it reached is destroyed, all before any durable copy existed anywhere and before the edge could learn whether its write succeeded, that event is unrecoverable — you cannot durably count data that was never durably recorded by anyone. This is a physical limit of any system on an unreliable network, not a gap in this design. The design is deliberately biased to fail toward **under-count** in this one irreducible case: every mechanism above (idempotent keys, conditional-insert-only, quorum-before-ack, disk- not memory-buffered retries) is chosen so that failure modes drop toward "miss" rather than "duplicate." That bias is a business decision, not an oversight — a missed impression is bounded revenue leakage (recoverable via SLA credit/estimation); a duplicated one is a billing-integrity and trust problem with an advertiser.
- **Single shard loses quorum:** blocked (unavailable), not incorrect, and scoped to that shard's key range only — upstream buffers retain data until it's writable again; no global outage.
- **Belt-and-suspenders:** continuous reconciliation between the live CRDT sum and the immutable-log batch replay catches implementation bugs; it is explicitly not what the "never over/under-count" guarantee rests on — that rests on the quorum+dedup invariant above.

## 4. Trade-offs, justified

**Rejected: single strongly-consistent counter (Spanner row / etcd key / Redis `INCR`).** Whether it's a single point of serialization (kills throughput) or gets sharded for throughput (at which point you've just rebuilt this architecture, minus the CRDT semantics and idempotency). The specific failure of naive sharded `INCR`: the operation itself isn't idempotent, so an ack-lost retry double-counts — the actual defect isn't "no CRDT," it's "no dedup key," which this design fixes directly.

**Rejected: increment-on-receipt with at-least-once delivery.** Simplest possible design; retries after ack-loss double-count, drops under-count — directly violates the hard requirement. Fixed by redefining the unit of work from "apply a mutation" to "record a uniquely-keyed fact."

**Rejected: pure fire-and-forget replication (ack locally, replicate async, hope it clears before a region dies).** Cheapest and lowest-latency, but it removes the retry signal that makes the zero-loss argument work: the client already got an ack, so it will never resend, and if the un-replicated tail is lost in a regional failure, that under-count is silent and permanent, not bounded and self-healing. This is the one alternative that would actually violate "never under-count" as a matter of course, not just in an edge case — rejected for that reason despite being simpler.

**Rejected: 2PC per event across regions.** Correctness-wise plausible, but a 2PC coordinator is itself a new single point of failure that must survive the exact regional failure the whole system is designed around, and per-event cross-region round trips at 1M/sec don't pipeline the same way a replicated log does.

**Rejected: HyperLogLog / probabilistic distinct-counting.** Right tool for approximate cardinality analytics; wrong tool here by construction, since billing requires exactness, not a ~1-2% error bound.

**Accepted cost: synchronous quorum commit before ack.** Adds real per-commit latency (~10-20ms with pod-scoping) to the tracking/billing path. This is affordable specifically because (1) it lands on a background confirmation path, not user-facing ad-render latency, and (2) pipelining plus client-side micro-batching means this is a latency cost, not a throughput ceiling — the math above shows a handful of shards, not tens of thousands, comfortably clears 1M/sec even without aggressive batching.

**Accepted cost: TTL-bounded live dedup index.** Unbounded retention of every `event_id` forever is wasteful and unnecessary; bounding it to the maximum plausible client retry window (24-48h) keeps the live store in the single-digit-terabyte range. This is safe only because the billing batch job performs a second, unbounded dedup pass over the full closed period as the actual correctness backstop — the TTL is a live-path optimization, never a correctness dependency.

**Accepted, deliberate bias: under-count over over-count in the one irreducible correlated-failure case.** Already justified above — it's the only place in the design where the two guarantees can't both be made airtight, and the direction chosen is the one with the lower business/legal cost.

**Why pod-scoped quorum instead of either a single region or a fully global quorum.** A single region can't survive a full region failure by definition. A quorum spread across distant regions (e.g., one member per continent) would work correctness-wise but forces every commit to pay full inter-continental RTT (150-300ms) and couples every write's success to the health of far-away, operationally unrelated regions. A small pod of nearby regions (~10-20ms RTT) gives the same "survive one full region loss" guarantee at a fraction of the latency and blast radius, with multiple independent pods worldwide providing global coverage.

**Bottom line:** treat impressions as immutable, idempotently-keyed facts rather than mutations to a shared counter; make dedup and durability the same atomic quorum-committed write, scoped to a small nearby-region pod so it's cheap and pipelines to well past 1M/sec; aggregate globally via a CRDT G-Counter that can't be corrupted by replay or reordering; and compute the number that must never be wrong from a watermarked replay of the immutable log at period close, never from the live counter. The only number the system can't make airtight is the one case where every copy of an event and the source that could retry it are destroyed simultaneously before durability exists anywhere — an irreducible limit of any system on an unreliable network, not a gap in this one — and the design is deliberately built to fail toward under-count, never over-count, in that single remaining sliver of risk.

# Globally-Distributed Ad-Impression Billing Counter

## The reframing that makes this solvable

"1M increments/sec, survive a full region loss, never over- or under-count" is unsatisfiable if you read it literally as *one shared integer that everyone safely `+1`s*. A single linearizable counter is a single hot key, which means a single consensus-group leader that all writes serialize through. Two independent walls stop it at 1M/sec:

- **The hot-key wall.** One Raft/Paxos leader tops out around 10k–100k log appends/sec (CPU/fsync/NIC bound) no matter how you pipeline — already >10× short of 1M/sec.
- **The serialization wall.** If reads are linearizable and an increment is read-modify-write, each op waits a full commit round, so throughput collapses to ~`1/RTT`. At 50–150 ms inter-region RTT that's **~7–20 ops/sec** (single digits if you need two round trips). To reach 1M/sec that way you'd need ~66,000 shards *and* still pay WAN latency on every write — i.e., you'd have re-derived a sharded design, badly.

So sharding is forced by physics, not taste. But the moment you shard, "one linearizable number" is gone. The resolution is to stop counting *mutations* and start counting *facts*:

> **The count is `|distinct durably-recorded impression IDs|`, derived by set-union, not by racing `+1`s.**

Because retries are *mandatory* in any system that survives failures (a client that doesn't get an ack must resend), and retries are what cause double-counting, exactness is impossible unless each impression is an immutable, idempotently-keyed event. This is the published-and-proven pattern behind Google's ads-billing stack (Photon's cross-region dedup registry over a Paxos-replicated ID store; F1/Spanner built for AdWords). We follow it.

The second reframing: **there is no single number.** Three consumers have three different needs, backed by the *same* durable facts but read on different paths:

| Consumer | Needs | Guarantee it gets |
|---|---|---|
| Pacing / frequency capping | Low latency, regional view, slight staleness OK | AP, sub-second |
| Dashboards / analytics | Seconds-to-minutes lag OK | Strong Eventual Consistency |
| **Billing / invoicing** | **Exact, auditable, correct at period close** — *not* real-time | Exact, from replayed ledger |

Only billing carries the "never over/under-count" requirement, and it explicitly does **not** need to be real-time. Optimizing the exact path for correctness-at-close instead of correctness-at-every-instant is what makes every requirement individually achievable.

## Consistency model

Three layers, each with the weakest guarantee that still does its job:

**1. Idempotency gate — CP (linearizable), globally sharded.**
The only place we pay for consensus. A horizontally sharded, multi-region-replicated strongly-consistent store (Spanner / CockroachDB / FoundationDB class). Sharded by `hash(event_id) mod N` (N ≈ few thousand); **each shard is its own consensus group of 3–5 replicas across distinct regions/failure domains**, majority quorum. The operation is a single atomic **conditional insert — `INSERT event_id IF NOT EXISTS`** — that commits only when a cross-region majority acks. First insert of an ID wins and counts; every later arrival of that ID is a no-op. Dedup and durable commit are *the same atomic operation*, so there is no window between "checked" and "recorded" in which a crash could double-count or drop. Because sharding is by `hash(event_id)`, the *same* ID always routes to the *same* consensus group regardless of which region a retry lands in — so dedup stays authoritative across regional failover, which is exactly the scenario we must survive.

**2. Aggregate counter — AP / Strong Eventual Consistency, via G-Counter CRDT.**
Every successful (first-seen) insert bumps one grow-only register. The global figure is a **G-Counter**: a vector with one entry per shard, each shard the sole writer of its own entry; **value = sum of entries, merge = element-wise max**. Max-merge is commutative, associative, and *idempotent*, so replays, reordering, partial merges, or re-delivery from a recovering region can never lose or double a count — replicas that have seen the same commits converge to the identical value. No coordination is needed to read a global total; any region answers from its local replica. (Shipping deltas with a monotonic per-shard offset is an equivalent formulation; state-based max-merge is simpler to reason about.)

**3. Billing truth — the reconciled ledger, not the live counter.**
The invoiced number is computed by a windowed batch job that replays the deduplicated, immutable event log for a *closed* (watermarked) period and takes `COUNT(DISTINCT event_id)` per advertiser/campaign. The live CRDT counter is a best-effort operational artifact; the ledger is the source of truth. This is the CAP-honest split: the sliver that must never be wrong (dedup) is CP and pays latency; everything downstream is AP and cheap, because idempotent CRDT merges physically cannot corrupt an already-correct set of facts.

**The load-bearing invariant tying it together:** *the client is acked only after the event's idempotency record has committed to a cross-region majority quorum.* Not after local/AZ durability — after cross-region durability. This is what makes "acked ⇒ survives any single-region loss" true, and it is the one place teams get this wrong.

## Data path

```
Ad server wins auction, serves the ad to the user immediately
   │  (ad rendering is NOT blocked on anything below)
   ▼
[1] Mint event_id = hash(auction_id + creative_id + publisher_id + server_nonce)
      • server-generated, exactly once, at the winning ad server
      • client/browser beacons are unreliable (double-fire, replay) → carriers of the ID only, never sources
   ▼
[2] Append {event_id, campaign_id, event_time, region} to nearest regional durable log
      (Kafka/Kinesis-class), acks=all, min.insync.replicas≥2 across 3 AZs
      • absorbs the 1M/sec write spike with pure horizontal partitioning, no coordination
   ▼
[3] Stream processor (Flink-class, exactly-once checkpointing) consumes the log and issues the
    conditional insert into the sharded cross-region consensus store  ── THE IDEMPOTENCY GATE
      • commit = cross-region majority quorum
      • the client's billing-record ack is gated on THIS commit (client retries same event_id until it gets it)
   ▼
[4] First-seen insert increments that shard's G-Counter register (local, lock-free)
   ▼
[5] Regional aggregator sums local registers → near-real-time regional total (feeds pacing)
    Async cross-region gossip of per-shard registers every 1–5s → every region holds the full
    vector → sum = eventually-consistent global total (feeds dashboards)
   ▼
[6] Immutable audit log: every raw tuple compacted from Kafka to multi-region object storage,
    long retention (dispute resolution, "why was this charged" months later)
   ▼
[7] Billing finalization: windowed batch (watermarked) closes each period once no further retries
    are possible (bounded by max client retry TTL, e.g. 48h). COUNT(DISTINCT event_id) over the
    deduped ledger = the invoiced number. Events arriving after the watermark post as a correction
    to the NEXT period — a closed invoice is never silently reopened (standard ad-billing practice).
```

Two things worth making explicit, because they defuse the usual objections:

- **Serving latency ≠ billing-commit latency.** The impression is shown to the user with zero added latency. The cross-region quorum commit (tens–low-hundreds of ms) happens on the *billing record*, off the ad-rendering critical path. The ad server holds the event and retries in the background until it gets a cross-region-durable ack.
- **The consensus store is not the throughput bottleneck.** Kafka absorbs the 1M/sec burst; the gate does dedup at its own sharded pace. Synchronous replication adds *latency per commit, not a throughput ceiling*, because commits are pipelined (don't wait for batch N's ack before proposing N+1) and spread across thousands of independent shards.

*(Optional micro-optimization: a per-shard Bloom filter as a fast **negative** pre-check. A Bloom negative means "definitely new" → skip the store lookup on the overwhelmingly common not-a-duplicate path. A Bloom positive must always be verified against the authoritative store, since a false positive would drop a genuinely new event and undercount. Never treat the Bloom filter as truth.)*

## Failure handling

**AZ failure within a region.** Invisible to correctness. Intra-region sync replication (ISR/Raft) rides it out; at worst a brief leader-election blip.

**Full region failure — the headline requirement.** Two invariants, each simple and verifiable, cover it — the guarantee does *not* rest on the region surviving:
- *Committed data always has a surviving copy.* An acked event committed to a cross-region majority, so losing one region still leaves the data (and the shard keeps writing in degraded 2-of-3 mode; a standby region is added and caught up later). **Zero loss of acked data.**
- *Uncommitted data is always retried.* If an event hadn't reached quorum, the client never got an ack — so its own retry-on-timeout logic resends it, *with the same `event_id`*, to a healthy region. Same ID → same shard → the surviving replicas record it exactly once.

So under a full region loss you lose neither committed nor in-flight events; the exposure is a sub-second-to-low-seconds *coverage gap* during failover, not data loss.

**"Never over-count" is the strictly stronger guarantee here, by design.** Because every write is a conditional insert keyed on `event_id`, retries, region-recovery reconciliation, and a retry that briefly lands in two regions during rerouting all collapse to one. Over-counting is structurally impossible regardless of how messy the failure/retry sequence gets. We make it the stronger guarantee deliberately: over-charging an advertiser is a trust/legal/refund liability, whereas a bounded under-count is recoverable.

**Regional network partition (region alive but isolated).** Ingest keeps accepting and buffers to the local durable log (AP); the cross-region dedup commits for that region simply stall — CP means it produces *no answer*, never a *wrong* answer — and drain on reconnect. Other shards are unaffected.

**Single dedup shard loses quorum.** Only that key range is blocked (unavailable, not incorrect); upstream Kafka retains its data until the shard is writable again. A thin slice of keys, never a global outage.

**Region recovery.** Standard consensus catch-up from the surviving replicas. No special-case recovery code, because the mechanism is uniform in steady state and under failure — a property worth protecting, since bespoke recovery paths are where correctness bugs breed.

**The one honest limit.** If an impression's billing record was never durably committed *anywhere* **and** the originating ad server also dies before it can retry, that event is lost. This is not an engineering gap — it's the Two Generals' boundary: you cannot count a fact that was never reliably transmitted to any survivor. When this unavoidable sliver occurs, **we bias to under-count, never over-count** (revenue leakage is recoverable via SLA credits/estimation; a fabricated charge is a billing-integrity and legal problem). Every mechanism above — server-minted IDs, conditional-insert-only, quorum-before-ack — is chosen so failure modes fail toward "miss," not "duplicate."

**Backstop.** An hourly job reconciles `sum(live CRDT registers)` against the exact batch replay of the immutable ledger; drift beyond epsilon alerts. This catches *implementation bugs*, not the primary correctness mechanism — and no invoice is issued while a reconciliation mismatch is open.

## Trade-offs, each justified

- **Single global atomic counter — rejected.** Simple and obviously correct in isolation, but it's either one serialization point (can't hit 1M/sec, per the two walls above) or one failure domain (can't survive region loss). Sharding is mandatory, and sharding forbids single-number linearizability.
- **2PC / cross-region consensus per increment — rejected.** Correct but ~7–20 ops/sec per serialized key, and the coordinator is itself a failure point that must survive the very region loss we're designing for. We pay consensus only at the idempotency gate, sharded thousands of ways, so it's a *latency* cost per request, not a *throughput* ceiling.
- **Pure CRDT counters with no dedup gate — rejected.** Infinitely scalable and fully AP, but the retries that durability *requires* would double-count without upstream idempotency. This is the version that actually violates "never over-count."
- **Fire-and-forget local ack, replicate later — rejected.** Cheapest and fastest, but a region loss then takes the un-replicated tail *with no retry signal* (the client was already acked), turning under-count into an unbounded, silent risk instead of a self-healing one. This is why the ack must wait for cross-region quorum.
- **Client/browser-generated IDs — rejected.** Beacons double-fire and replay; trusting them as ID *sources* corrupts dedup. IDs are minted server-side, once; the beacon only carries them.
- **Store every event_id forever for live dedup — rejected.** Unbounded. The live gate TTLs its dedup state at the max plausible retry window (24–48h); the batch billing job does a second, *unbounded* DISTINCT pass over the immutable log, so the TTL is a cost optimization, never a correctness dependency.
- **Billing off the live counter — deliberately not done.** The live number is eventually consistent and can be transiently stale after a partition. Advertisers don't need sub-second billing accuracy; they need *correct daily/monthly totals*. Spending exactness to buy real-time-ness nobody asked for is a bad trade, so the invoiced number always comes from the replayed ledger.
- **Consensus-group placement — tunable.** 3 replicas in nearby regions (~10–20 ms RTT) minimize commit latency and survive any single-region loss; spreading replicas across continents (or going to 5 replicas) buys stronger fault-independence at higher latency. Pick per durability SLA.

## Capacity check

- 1M events/sec × ~300 B ≈ **300 MB/s** ingest, **86.4 B events/day**.
- ~4,000 dedup shards → **~250 commits/sec/shard** — trivial for a pipelined consensus group even across regions, and no hot shard because keys hash uniformly.
- Cross-region commit at ~100 ms ⇒ only **~100k events in flight** globally at any instant — negligible client-side buffering.

**Bottom line:** exactness comes from *idempotent conditional-insert + quorum-before-ack* at the write path (a bounded, per-shard problem that's cheap to make correct); scale comes from *massive sharding + G-Counter summation* (an aggregation problem that's cheap to make fast); the two are joined by treating the live global figure as an operationally useful approximation and the periodically-replayed immutable ledger as the actual billing ground truth. The only unrecoverable loss is the physically-impossible-to-avoid one, and it is deliberately biased toward under-counting.
