# Globally-Distributed Exact Ad-Impression Counter

## 0. The reframing that makes this solvable

"1M increments/sec, survive a full region failure, never over- or under-count" is unsolvable if you think of it as *one mutable integer that many writers race to update*. A single linearizable register — Spanner row, etcd key, Redis `INCR` — forces every writer through one serialization point. Sharding that register fixes throughput but breaks the idea of "one true number," and a naive back-of-envelope (RTT-bound, no pipelining) makes cross-region synchronous replication look impossibly slow, which tempts people into pure fire-and-forget replication — which then silently loses or duplicates data under retries.

The move that resolves all of this at once: stop modeling impressions as *increments applied to a counter* and start modeling them as **immutable, uniquely-identified events**, where "the count" is `|deduplicated events successfully recorded|`. Once counting is redefined as **set cardinality of a durable, idempotently-keyed log** rather than *arithmetic on a shared mutable value*, retries, replays, reordering, and partial failures stop being correctness threats — a duplicate delivery of the same event is just a no-op. This is the same pattern production ad-serving systems actually use (Google's Photon/MillWheel-style exactly-once stream joining; Spanner's original production workload was an ads-billing backend for exactly this reason) — it isn't a novel trick, it's the standard answer to this exact problem.

The second move: **stop trying to make one number serve every reader.** Three different consumers need this count, with genuinely different consistency requirements:

| Consumer | Needs | Consistency |
|---|---|---|
| Budget pacing / frequency capping | Low latency, regional view, slight staleness tolerable (industry-standard soft caps with a buffer) | Fast, local, approximate |
| Dashboards / analytics | Global view, seconds-to-minutes lag fine | Eventually consistent |
| Billing / invoicing | Must be **exact**, but only needs to be correct **at period close**, not in real time | Exact, delayed |

Only the billing consumer has the hard "never over/under-count" requirement. Design for that one explicitly; the other two ride on the same durable data for free, at near-zero extra cost.

## 1. Consistency model

**Write/durability boundary — linearizable, CP, but geographically scoped.** Impressions are hash-partitioned across many shards. Each shard is a **synchronously-replicated, consensus-backed key-value range** (Raft/Paxos — e.g., a CockroachDB/FoundationDB/Spanner-style range, or a purpose-built Raft shard), with replicas placed across **a small "pod" of 3 geographically nearby regions** (same continent, ~10–20ms RTT) — not 2, since majority quorum over 2 replicas requires both to be up, so losing either one stalls the shard; 3 is the minimum pod size that actually tolerates a full region loss — and not scattered globally. A write is a single **conditional put** — `INSERT event_id IF NOT EXISTS` — and it is not acknowledged to the caller until it has **quorum-committed (majority of the pod)**. Dedup and durability are the *same atomic operation*, not two systems wired together — that's what closes off the classic check-then-write race and the "acked locally, lost before it reaches durable multi-region storage" gap that a two-hop design (fast local buffer → async promotion to a separate global store) would reopen. If you ack before the durability boundary you actually care about, you've quietly rebuilt the fire-and-forget design this whole exercise exists to avoid.

**Aggregation layer — Strong Eventual Consistency via CRDT.** Each shard exposes a monotonically-increasing local count (successful conditional-puts). The global total for any window is the sum of all shard counts — a **G-Counter** (grow-only CRDT): merge is commutative, associative, and idempotent, so out-of-order delivery, replays, or partial gossip between pods can never double-count or diverge, only converge. No coordination is needed to compute a global sum: any region can answer "current total" from whatever shard deltas it has locally received.

**Billing truth = reconciled batch replay, not the live counter.** The invoiced number is never read off the live CRDT sum. It's computed by a windowed batch job that closes each period only after a **watermark** confirms no more late arrivals are expected, then replays the deduplicated, immutable event log for that exact window. The live number is a best-effort operational artifact; the append-only log is the ledger of record.

This is a deliberate, CAP-theorem-honest split: the narrow slice that must never be wrong (the conditional-put dedup/durability gate) pays consensus latency and is CP; everything downstream — aggregation, dashboards, pacing — is AP/SEC and effectively free, because idempotent CRDT merges cannot corrupt a set of facts that's already correct.

## 2. Data path

```
Ad-serving decision (server-side, at auction/render time)
  → mint event_id ONCE, server-side (hash of auction_id+creative_id+publisher_id+nonce,
     or UUIDv7), embed it as a literal value in the tracking pixel/beacon URL
  → client/SDK only ever ECHOES that id back — never generates or recomputes it
       [critical: if the client computed its own id per beacon-fire, a client-side
        double-fire bug would mint two different ids for one real impression and
        dedup-by-id would silently miss it]
  → edge/ad-server buffers to LOCAL DISK (not memory) before any network call
  → micro-batches events by destination shard (batch ~1-10K events / ~5-10ms linger,
     the same batch.size+linger.ms pattern every Kafka producer already uses)
  → sends batch to nearest quorum-capable pod, pipelines many batches concurrently
     (don't block batch N+1 on batch N's ack)
  → shard = Raft/Paxos range replicated across a 3-region pod, 2-of-3 quorum
  → conditional put per event_id (create-if-absent) is the SAME operation as the
     durability commit
  → ack to edge ONLY after quorum commit  →  edge marks batch confirmed, drops from
     local disk buffer
  → on timeout/no-ack, edge retries the identical batch/ids to another reachable
     member of the SAME pod (never a different pod — that pod doesn't hold this
     shard's Raft/KV state) — safe, because dedup collapses retries to one count
     regardless of which member of the pod eventually processes it. If no member
     of the pod is reachable, the shard is unavailable, not rerouted (see §3)
  → committed shard log is continuously exported (changefeed/CDC) to immutable
     object storage (S3/GCS) — the audit/dispute-resolution ground truth
  → per-shard G-Counter deltas gossip asynchronously across pods (~1-5s target lag)
     → any region sums its local vector of shard counts → global live/pacing/
        dashboard total, no cross-region read required
  → at period close: watermarked batch job (Spark/Flink) replays the closed
     window's log, deduplicating both within the window AND against a permanent,
     append-only archive of every event_id already billed in any prior period
     (id-only, not full payload — see §2) → this count, not the live counter, is
     what's invoiced. An id already present in that archive (e.g., a legitimate
     retry that outlived the live TTL and only landed after the window closed) is
     recognized as a duplicate of an already-billed event and dropped, never
     re-billed. Only genuinely new ids post as a correction to the NEXT period's
     ledger, and the archive is extended with this period's billed ids before
     close — never reopen a closed invoice.
  → reconciliation: batch replay count vs. live CRDT sum diffed continuously;
     mismatch beyond epsilon pages on-call (this catches bugs; it is not the
     primary correctness mechanism, which is the quorum+dedup gate itself)
```

**Why this clears 1M/sec despite synchronous cross-region commits.** Synchronous replication adds *latency per commit*, not a *ceiling on aggregate throughput*, as long as commits are pipelined — the same principle (Little's Law: concurrency = throughput × latency) that lets TCP and every production Raft/Paxos implementation sustain high throughput over long-RTT links. Concretely, at the ~10–20ms pod-internal RTT this pod-scoping achieves: with **no batching at all**, ~100 concurrent in-flight commits per shard already sustains 5,000–10,000 events/sec/shard, so only ~100–200 shards are needed to clear 1M/sec on throughput grounds alone. With realistic client-side micro-batching (e.g., 1,000 events per commit, ~100 batches pipelined), a *single* shard can clear >1M events/sec in isolation — meaning shard count is set well above this throughput minimum, as a resilience/blast-radius dial: e.g., a few thousand (~3,000) shards, so any one shard's outage or replay affects only a small slice of the key space, not a throughput necessity. A design that assumes each shard is stuck at "1 write per round-trip" (implying tens of thousands of shards) is solving the wrong problem; that ceiling only applies if you refuse to pipeline, which no real consensus implementation does. Pod-scoping (3 *nearby* regions, not a globally-spread quorum) is what keeps that per-commit RTT to ~10–20ms instead of ~150–300ms — it's also what keeps every write's success from being coupled to the health of a distant, unrelated region.

**Storage is trivial at this scale.** Bounding the live dedup index to a 24–48h TTL (the max plausible span of client-side disk-buffered retries) costs on the order of ten terabytes at most (~7–14TB across the full key space at 1M/sec sustained) — single digits at the 24h end, low double digits at the 48h end. Spread across that same ~3,000-shard operational count set above (chosen for blast-radius, not throughput), that's ~2.3–4.7GB per shard — under 5GB, and consistent with the shard count actually in play, not the ~100–200-shard throughput minimum. The TTL bound is an optimization, not a correctness dependency: the real backstop is a *cross-period* archive of billed `event_id`s, checked by every period-close batch job, that is unbounded in time rather than scoped to the single closed window — this is what actually catches a retry that outlives the live TTL during a prolonged region-isolation event and only resurfaces in a later billing period. Kept as raw ids this would still grow ~1.4TB/day at 1M/sec sustained; to keep it cheap indefinitely, periods older than a rolling horizon (e.g., 90 days) are compacted from exact ids into a Bloom filter — no false negatives, so it never *misses* a real duplicate, only (rarely) flags a genuinely new event as one. That failure mode pushes toward under-count, the direction this design already accepts as the lesser risk (§3), so the archive's correctness guarantee for "never over-count" holds unconditionally while its storage cost stays bounded.

**Optional fast path:** a Bloom filter per shard can short-circuit the common "definitely new" case (Bloom filters have no false negatives, so "definitely not seen" is always trustworthy) to skip the exact-lookup cost — but it must never be used as the source of truth, since a false positive ("possibly seen") would silently cause an undercount if trusted directly.

## 3. Failure handling

- **AZ failure inside a region:** invisible to correctness — standard Raft/ISR leader re-election inside the pod; brief latency blip only.
- **Full failure of one region in a pod:** the surviving 2-of-3 still form quorum, so the shard keeps accepting writes uninterrupted in degraded (under-replicated) mode. Traffic destined for the dead region fails over via anycast/GSLB to a surviving member of the *same* pod (never a different pod — a shard's dedup/consensus state lives only in its designated pod, so routing a retry to an unrelated pod would either silently drop it or create an un-reconciled second commit). A standby replica is added and catches up once a healthy region is available. If a correlated failure takes out the whole pod (2+ of 3 regions simultaneously — beyond the single-region-loss guarantee this pod size targets), the shard simply becomes unavailable rather than failing over elsewhere: see "single shard loses quorum" below. No special-case recovery path otherwise — steady-state and failure-recovery use the identical Raft catch-up mechanism.
- **Region isolated but alive (network partition, not a crash):** it can't reach its pod-mates, so it can't independently form quorum and must not accept writes as if it could (that would reintroduce the ack-before-true-durability bug) — clients reroute to a reachable quorum-capable member of that same pod instead, exactly as in a full failure. The isolated region simply falls behind and catches up on reconnection.
- **The zero-loss argument, stated as an invariant, not a hope:** at the instant of any failure, every in-flight event is in exactly one of two states. (a) It quorum-committed — by definition ≥2 regions hold it, so losing 1 region loses zero committed data. (b) It did not quorum-commit — by definition the edge never received an ack, so the edge's own retry logic (disk-backed, same `event_id`) resends it to another reachable member of the *same* pod (a full-pod outage instead blocks the shard, above, rather than rerouting), where dedup collapses the retry to exactly one count. "Never under-count" therefore doesn't depend on any specific region surviving; it depends only on (a) committed data always having a surviving quorum copy and (b) an edge that never got an ack always retrying against that shard's one pod, however long it takes. "Never over-count" is structurally guaranteed independent of failure timing: within the live TTL window every retry — however many times or however many pod members it's sent to — is gated by the same conditional-put-on-`event_id`; retries that outlive the TTL are caught instead by the cross-period archive backstop (§2), so no failure-timing window exists where a duplicate can slip through uncounted as new.
- **The one genuinely irreducible edge case:** if the edge device itself is destroyed *and* every region it reached is destroyed, all before any durable copy existed anywhere and before the edge could learn whether its write succeeded, that event is unrecoverable — you cannot durably count data that was never durably recorded by anyone. This is a physical limit of any system on an unreliable network, not a gap in this design. The design is deliberately biased to fail toward **under-count** in this one irreducible case: every mechanism above (idempotent keys, conditional-insert-only, quorum-before-ack, disk- not memory-buffered retries) is chosen so that failure modes drop toward "miss" rather than "duplicate." That bias is a business decision, not an oversight — a missed impression is bounded revenue leakage (recoverable via SLA credit/estimation); a duplicated one is a billing-integrity and trust problem with an advertiser.
- **Single shard loses quorum:** blocked (unavailable), not incorrect, and scoped to that shard's key range only — upstream buffers retain data until it's writable again; no global outage.
- **Belt-and-suspenders:** continuous reconciliation between the live CRDT sum and the immutable-log batch replay catches implementation bugs; it is explicitly not what the "never over/under-count" guarantee rests on — that rests on the quorum+dedup invariant above.

## 4. Trade-offs, justified

**Rejected: single strongly-consistent counter (Spanner row / etcd key / Redis `INCR`).** Whether it's a single point of serialization (kills throughput) or gets sharded for throughput (at which point you've just rebuilt this architecture, minus the CRDT semantics and idempotency). The specific failure of naive sharded `INCR`: the operation itself isn't idempotent, so an ack-lost retry double-counts — the actual defect isn't "no CRDT," it's "no dedup key," which this design fixes directly.

**Rejected: increment-on-receipt with at-least-once delivery.** Simplest possible design; retries after ack-loss double-count, drops under-count — directly violates the hard requirement. Fixed by redefining the unit of work from "apply a mutation" to "record a uniquely-keyed fact."

**Rejected: pure fire-and-forget replication (ack locally, replicate async, hope it clears before a region dies).** Cheapest and lowest-latency, but it removes the retry signal that makes the zero-loss argument work: the client already got an ack, so it will never resend, and if the un-replicated tail is lost in a regional failure, that under-count is silent and permanent, not bounded and self-healing. This is the one alternative that would actually violate "never under-count" as a matter of course, not just in an edge case — rejected for that reason despite being simpler.

**Rejected: 2PC per event across regions.** Correctness-wise plausible, but a 2PC coordinator is itself a new single point of failure that must survive the exact regional failure the whole system is designed around, and per-event cross-region round trips at 1M/sec don't pipeline the same way a replicated log does.

**Rejected: HyperLogLog / probabilistic distinct-counting.** Right tool for approximate cardinality analytics; wrong tool here by construction, since billing requires exactness, not a ~1-2% error bound.

**Accepted cost: synchronous quorum commit before ack.** Adds real per-commit latency (~10-20ms with pod-scoping) to the tracking/billing path. This is affordable specifically because (1) it lands on a background confirmation path, not user-facing ad-render latency, and (2) pipelining plus client-side micro-batching means this is a latency cost, not a throughput ceiling — the math above shows on the order of a couple hundred shards, not tens of thousands, comfortably clears 1M/sec even without aggressive batching.

**Accepted cost: TTL-bounded live dedup index, backed by a cross-period archive.** Unbounded *exact* retention of every `event_id` in the hot live store forever is wasteful and unnecessary; bounding the live store to the maximum plausible client retry window (24-48h) keeps it in the single-digit-to-low-double-digit-terabyte range (~7–14TB). This is safe only because the actual correctness backstop is a separate, genuinely unbounded-in-time archive of billed ids spanning *all* prior periods, not just the closed window being reconciled — without that cross-period scope, a retry that outlives the TTL during a prolonged region-isolation event would land in a later period looking like a new event and over-count. To keep that archive itself cheap indefinitely, older periods are compacted into Bloom filters (§2), whose only failure mode (false-positive "seen") biases toward under-count — consistent with, not in tension with, the deliberate bias already accepted below. The live TTL is a live-path latency/storage optimization; the cross-period archive, not the single-window batch pass alone, is the correctness dependency.

**Accepted, deliberate bias: under-count over over-count in the one irreducible correlated-failure case.** Already justified above — it's the only place in the design where the two guarantees can't both be made airtight, and the direction chosen is the one with the lower business/legal cost.

**Why pod-scoped quorum instead of either a single region or a fully global quorum.** A single region can't survive a full region failure by definition. A quorum spread across distant regions (e.g., one member per continent) would work correctness-wise but forces every commit to pay full inter-continental RTT (150-300ms) and couples every write's success to the health of far-away, operationally unrelated regions. A small pod of 3 nearby regions (~10-20ms RTT) gives the same "survive one full region loss" guarantee at a fraction of the latency and blast radius, with multiple independent pods worldwide providing global coverage.

**Bottom line:** treat impressions as immutable, idempotently-keyed facts rather than mutations to a shared counter; make dedup and durability the same atomic quorum-committed write, scoped to a small 3-region nearby pod so it's cheap and pipelines to well past 1M/sec; aggregate globally via a CRDT G-Counter that can't be corrupted by replay or reordering; and compute the number that must never be wrong from a watermarked replay of the immutable log, cross-checked against a permanent archive of every previously-billed id, at period close, never from the live counter. The only number the system can't make airtight is the one case where every copy of an event and the source that could retry it are destroyed simultaneously before durability exists anywhere — an irreducible limit of any system on an unreliable network, not a gap in this one — and the design is deliberately built to fail toward under-count, never over-count, in that single remaining sliver of risk.

answers.opusJudge:
## Why "exactly-once" is a marketing term

You cannot make "do the work" and "durably record that the work was done" a single atomic step when those two acts span a network (worker ↔ broker ↔ downstream store). Over a lossy channel no protocol guarantees both ends agree the message was handled exactly once — this is the Two Generals problem. So every queue that advertises exactly-once actually implements **at-least-once delivery + de-duplication/idempotency = "effectively once."** (Consensus among brokers doesn't rescue this: it can agree on *delivery*, but it can't un-charge a card or un-send an email — the external, non-idempotent side effect is outside the transaction.)

That reframes the symptom precisely. Seeing a job "processed twice" requires **two things to coincide**: (1) the message was *delivered* more than once, or two messages represent one job, **and** (2) the idempotency layer failed to collapse them into one *effect*. A duplicate delivery hitting a correctly idempotent handler is a non-event. So the distinct mechanisms fall into three families: the delivery layer redelivered, the dedup layer had a gap, or duplication was injected above the queue.

---

## Family 1 — The delivery layer legitimately delivered more than once (at-least-once doing its job)

**1. Visibility-timeout / lease expiry vs. a slow-but-alive worker**
Processing outlasts the invisibility window (SQS visibility timeout, Kafka `max.poll.interval.ms`, a Redis lock TTL) because of a GC pause, cold start, or downstream latency spike. The broker assumes the worker died and redelivers to a second worker; the first also finishes.
- *Detection:* same job_id run by two different worker/host IDs, with the second pickup landing ≈ `first_pickup + visibility_timeout`; `ApproximateReceiveCount` (SQS) or delivery-count > 1 while the first process is still alive; correlates with p99 runtime approaching the timeout.
- *Fix:* heartbeat-renew the lease while working; set the base timeout above p99.9 runtime and alert as it's approached; add **fencing tokens** (a monotonically increasing token issued at lease acquisition) so a zombie worker's late write is rejected downstream. A clock-skew variant — a host with a slow NTP clock believing an expired lease is still valid — has the same fix (fencing tokens beat wall-clock lease comparison across hosts).

**2. Crash/partition between "side effect committed" and "ack sent"**
The worker completes the real work, then crashes, is OOM-killed, or loses the network before the ack (delete/offset-commit/status-flip) lands. No ack ⇒ redelivery.
- *Detection:* redelivery with evidence attempt #1 actually completed (a result/log from attempt #1 exists before the ack gap); duplicate outputs, same job_id, distinct trace IDs; gap ≈ the timeout interval.
- *Fix:* make completion durable *atomically with the work* — write a `processed_jobs(job_id UNIQUE)` row (or the result under a unique constraint) in the **same transaction** as the side effect, or use a **transactional outbox**; the ack becomes advisory and the DB constraint is the source of truth.

**3. Producer-side duplicate enqueue (ambiguous publish)**
The producer publishes, the broker accepts it, but the broker's ack back to the producer is lost. The producer retries and creates a *second, distinct* message for one logical job. This is duplicate *enqueue* masquerading as duplicate *processing*.
- *Detection:* two **different** message IDs, same business payload/key, enqueued within one retry-timeout window; same producer request ID in logs.
- *Fix:* an idempotent producer (Kafka producer-ID + sequence number; SQS FIFO `MessageDeduplicationId`) or a business-derived idempotency key enforced by a unique constraint at ingestion. Critically, the key must be generated **once at the call site and reused across retries**, not regenerated per attempt.

**4. Consumer-group rebalance / zombie consumer**
A rebalance (deploy, autoscale, spot eviction) reassigns a partition mid-flight. The old consumer finishes and commits after the new owner has already reprocessed from the last committed offset. (Also: manual offset commit *after* processing that crashes before the commit lands ⇒ redelivery on the next rebalance. Note the opposite ordering — auto-commit *before* processing — causes message *loss*, not duplication.)
- *Detection:* duplicates cluster at deploy/scale-event timestamps; two consumer instances with different group-generation/epoch numbers touch the same offset; `CommitFailedException` correlated in time.
- *Fix:* commit per-message before starting the next unit; use cooperative (incremental) rebalancing and drain in-flight work in `onPartitionsRevoked`; generation/epoch fencing so a stale commit is rejected; for a pure consume-process-produce loop *within Kafka*, use Kafka EOS transactions (this does **not** extend exactly-once to arbitrary external systems).

**5. Broker split-brain / failover / stale-replica read**
A network partition or misconfigured consensus lets two broker/coordinator replicas each believe they're leader and dispatch the same job; or a claim-check reads a lagging replica that still shows the job unclaimed.
- *Detection:* duplicates correlate with cluster events (leader-election/failover logs), not with worker-side timing; two broker nodes log independent dispatch of the same job.
- *Fix:* quorum-based leader election (Raft/ZAB) with fencing/epoch numbers on writes; perform the claim/dedup read against the leader only (strong consistency), never a replica.

---

## Family 2 — Delivery was fine (or should have been caught) but the idempotency layer has a gap

**6. Non-atomic check-then-act (TOCTOU)**
Two overlapping deliveries both run `SELECT "processed?"` → false, both proceed, both write "processed." The check-then-act isn't atomic. Same bug in a DB-backed queue: `SELECT` eligible rows then a separate `UPDATE status='claimed'` lets two workers grab the same row.
- *Detection:* duplicates paired within milliseconds (true concurrency, not a timeout-length gap); logs show both workers reading `exists=false` before either wrote; reproducible under concurrent load.
- *Fix:* make the gate a single atomic conditional write — `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING`, `UPDATE ... WHERE status='pending' RETURNING *`, or `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`. Claim-and-read must be one statement.

**7. Dedup store TTL / eviction (durability gap)**
Idempotency is a cache entry (Redis `SETNX` with TTL, SQS FIFO's 5-minute dedup window, Kafka producer idempotence bound to the producer session/epoch). A redelivery arrives *after* the window closes, or the cache restarts/evicts under memory pressure.
- *Detection:* duplicates separated by a gap **larger** than the configured dedup TTL/window; surfaces under long backlogs, downstream outages, or after a producer restart (new epoch).
- *Fix:* back the check with a durable, business-lifetime store (a completed-jobs table with a unique constraint), with the cache as a front optimization only — never the sole gate.

**8. Idempotency record written after the external side effect (ordering gap)**
The handler calls a non-idempotent external API (charge, email), *then* records that it did. A crash in between makes redelivery repeat the external call even though internal bookkeeping looks clean.
- *Detection:* the external system shows two operations; the internal execution ledger shows one.
- *Fix:* write an "intent" record *before* the call, and pass a stable idempotency key to the external API (Stripe-style) derived from the business key — so the *receiver* dedups, not just the sender.

**9. No idempotency key on the effect, or dedup keyed on the wrong identifier**
Delivery is genuinely single, but the effect isn't guarded (blind `INSERT`, `counter += 1`, unconditional send); or dedup is keyed on the queue's internal message ID, which cannot catch the two-distinct-messages case from mechanism #3.
- *Detection:* one dequeue in queue logs but two downstream effects (distinguishes this from #1–#7, where you'd see two dequeues); or duplicates share a business key but differ in message ID despite "dedup enabled."
- *Fix:* make the terminal effect naturally idempotent (upsert, set-to-value) and key dedup on a **business-derived** identity, not the transport message ID.

---

## Family 3 — Duplication injected above/around the queue (queue delivery-count = 1)

**10. Application-level retry wrapper re-running the whole handler**
A retry/circuit-breaker decorator wraps the entire job function and catches an exception thrown *after* the real side effect succeeded (e.g., in a post-processing step), re-running everything.
- *Detection:* broker shows the message delivered exactly once, yet duplicate effects appear; app logs show an internal retry trace, not a redelivery.
- *Fix:* retry only the failed sub-step; checkpoint completed sub-steps so re-entry skips finished work; never wrap a non-idempotent effect in a blind "retry the whole function."

**11. Independent scheduler/orchestrator re-submits the logical job**
A cron/orchestration layer above the queue runs its own "did this run?" liveness check (missing heartbeat, stale lock) and submits a *fresh* job for the same logical work, bypassing the queue's dedup entirely.
- *Detection:* two distinct run_ids under one scheduled_id/job definition, each with a full execution trace.
- *Fix:* a distributed lock / leader election keyed on the logical job identity, with a TTL tied to real progress (heartbeats), plus the unique constraint at the results layer.

**12. Dual-write / fan-out / migration double-publish**
The same logical job is published to two queues or topics (old and new systems during a migration; a misconfigured fan-out), each consumed to completion.
- *Detection:* two distinct job IDs from two topics, identical payload, **no** crash/timeout correlation — steady-state duplication at a constant rate.
- *Fix:* a single publish path; if dual-write is temporarily required, dedup downstream on the business key and track cutover to remove the second path.

**Mechanism #1 (lease/visibility-timeout expiry against a slow-but-alive worker), compounded by a Family-2 gap** — most commonly a non-atomic (#6), non-durable (#7), or wrongly-ordered (#8) idempotency check, or no idempotency key at all (#9). #2 (crash-before-ack) is the close runner-up and shares the mechanism.

The reasoning:

- **It requires no failure whatsoever** — no crash, no partition, no split-brain — only ordinary runtime variance exceeding a static timeout. Every job with a fat right tail (GC pause, cold cache, slow downstream) is exposed on *every* run, so its rate scales with volume × P(runtime > timeout). That probability *rises under load* — exactly when duplicates get noticed. Every other redelivery trigger needs a specific event (a rebalance, a leader election, a millisecond-precise crash, a minutes-long backlog) that is rarer than "a worker took a little too long."
- **It's the documented default of every popular queue** (SQS, RabbitMQ, Celery/Redis, Kafka-with-manual-commit), all of which are at-least-once underneath and whose docs explicitly tell you to make consumers idempotent — a tacit admission this is the dominant residual case.
- **The compound is what turns a benign duplicate delivery into a duplicate effect.** Redelivery of the *same* job_id would be caught by a proper durable, atomic dedup — but teams routinely code the check as `SELECT` then `INSERT`, or as a cache with a TTL, or record success *after* the side effect. So the moment two deliveries overlap, the gap lets both through.

Stop trying to prevent duplicate delivery; make delivery count irrelevant. **Treat the queue as at-least-once by contract, attach a stable business-derived idempotency key to the job, and make the handler's terminal write a conditional upsert on that key (`INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (key) DO NOTHING`) committed in the same transaction that marks the job done** (or via a transactional outbox). Add **heartbeat lease renewal** so long-but-alive jobs aren't reassigned, and **fencing tokens** so a genuine zombie's late write is rejected downstream.

That combination neutralizes #1, #2, #6, #7, #8, and #9 at once, and it's strictly easier and more robust than trying to perfectly tune every timeout, rebalance window, and leader election. ## Why this happens at all

Exactly-once *delivery* isn't achievable in an asynchronous distributed system. A sender can never distinguish "the message never arrived" from "it arrived and only the acknowledgment was lost" — that's the Two Generals Problem. And a coordinator can never reliably distinguish "the worker died" from "the worker is just slow" without waiting an unbounded amount of time — that's the same slow-vs-dead ambiguity the FLP impossibility result formalizes. Because of this, every queue that advertises "exactly-once" is actually shipping **at-least-once delivery + idempotent/deduplicated processing** ("effectively-once"). A duplicate-processing incident means that idempotency layer has a gap. A redelivery by itself is normal and harmless; it only becomes a customer-visible duplicate when it *also* coincides with a gap in the dedup logic. Below are the distinct places either half can fail.

## Mechanisms

### 1. Visibility-timeout / lease expiry on a worker that's still alive
The consumer takes longer than the broker's invisibility window (SQS visibility timeout, Kafka `max.poll.interval.ms`, a Redis lock TTL) — a GC pause, a downstream latency spike, a cold start, noisy-neighbor CPU steal. The broker assumes the worker died and redelivers; a second worker completes the job, and then the first one finishes and commits anyway. A cross-host clock-skew variant produces the same effect: the lease is judged by wall-clock comparison, and a worker on a slow-drifting clock believes its lease is still valid after the broker has already reassigned it.
- **Detection:** the same job_id processed by two different worker/host IDs, with the second pickup landing almost exactly at `first_pickup + timeout`; SQS `ApproximateReceiveCount` > 1; correlates with p99 processing-latency spikes, GC/CPU pressure, or cold starts — not with any crash.
- **Fix:** heartbeat-based lease renewal (the worker actively extends its lease while still working) instead of a static timeout; set the base timeout comfortably above p99.9 duration. Add **fencing tokens** — a monotonically increasing token issued at lease acquisition, checked by every downstream write — so even a genuine zombie's late write is rejected. Fencing tokens close the clock-skew variant entirely, since they don't depend on wall-clock comparison at all.

### 2. Side effect completes, then a crash/partition loses the ack
The worker finishes the real work, then crashes, gets OOM-killed, or loses network before the ack/offset-commit/delete-message lands. The broker's default assumption is "no ack = not done," so it redelivers. The same pattern shows up at batch granularity — a worker pulls N messages, finishes several, then crashes before the batch-level ack/offset-commit, so the whole batch (including already-completed items) is redelivered — and is most dangerous when the side effect is a call to an external, non-idempotent system (charging a card, sending an email): if the crash lands between the external call succeeding and your own bookkeeping recording that fact, redelivery doesn't just re-run harmless internal logic, it repeats the external call.
- **Detection:** duplicate side effects close together in time; the first worker's logs end right after "work done" with no matching "ack sent" entry; broker shows redelivery shortly after. For the external-call variant, the external system's own logs (payment processor, email provider) show two operations where your internal job log shows only one. For the batch variant, duplicates cluster at batch boundaries and correlate with a mid-batch crash/restart.
- **Fix:** make completion durable and atomic *with* the work — write to a `processed_jobs(job_id UNIQUE)` table (or a transactional-outbox pattern) in the same local transaction as the side effect, then ack; the ack becomes advisory, the DB constraint is the real source of truth. For external calls, also write a "pending intent" record *before* making the call (not just a completion record after), and pass a stable, business-derived idempotency key to the external API itself wherever it's supported (Stripe-style idempotency keys), keyed on your own business identifier rather than the queue's internal message ID — so even a gap in your own crash recovery still gets deduped by the receiver. For batches, commit/ack per completed item, not once for the whole batch.

### 3. Non-atomic check-then-act (TOCTOU) in the claim or the idempotency check
Either the queue is a DB table where a consumer does a `SELECT` for eligible rows followed by a separate `UPDATE status='claimed'` (two workers can both `SELECT` the same row before either updates it), or the idempotency check itself is a plain "has this run? → no → run it → mark it run" sequence that isn't atomic. Two workers can pass the "not yet processed" check before either has recorded that it's processing.
- **Detection:** two executions with near-identical timestamps (true concurrency, not a timeout-driven redelivery gap); logs show both workers reading `exists=false`/`unclaimed` before either wrote; reproducible under concurrent load.
- **Fix:** replace read-then-write with a single atomic conditional statement — `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (job_id) DO NOTHING`, a unique constraint, or `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` / `UPDATE ... WHERE status='pending' RETURNING *` for DB-backed queues. The atomic statement becomes the actual gate for whether work proceeds, not a check that precedes it.

### 4. Producer-side duplicate enqueue (publish-ack loss)
The producer publishes, the broker accepts it, but the ack back to the producer times out or is lost. The producer retries, believing the publish never landed, creating a second, distinct message/job ID for the same logical unit of work. Both copies are legitimately delivered exactly once each — the duplication happened at enqueue time, not at delivery time.
- **Detection:** two *different* message/job IDs, identical business payload or idempotency key, enqueued within one retry-timeout window of each other, often sharing a producer request ID in logs.
- **Fix:** a stable, business-derived idempotency key generated once at the call site — never regenerated on each retry attempt — enforced with a unique constraint at ingestion, plus transport-level idempotent producers where available (Kafka producer-ID + per-partition sequence number, SQS FIFO `MessageDeduplicationId`). Note that transport-level dedup only catches retries of the *same* send call; it does nothing if the application generates a fresh ID per attempt.

### 5. Dedup store is a cache with a TTL/window, not a durable record
Idempotency is implemented as a bounded structure — a Redis key with an expiry, SQS FIFO's 5-minute dedup interval, or Kafka idempotent-producer state tied to the producer's session/epoch (a producer restart gets a new producer ID, silently resetting the dedup window). A redelivery or retry arriving after that window closes, or after the store itself restarted/evicted under memory pressure, looks brand new.
- **Detection:** the duplicate arrives after a gap longer than the configured TTL/window; correlates with a queue backlog, downstream outage, delayed retry, or a producer/cache restart.
- **Fix:** back the check with a durable, effectively unbounded store — a completed-jobs table with a unique constraint on job_id — and treat any cache in front of it purely as a fast-path optimization, never the sole gate.

### 6. Consumer-group rebalance mid-processing (zombie member)
A rebalance — new pod deployed, autoscaler event, pod eviction — reassigns a partition to a different consumer while the original member hasn't committed its offset yet. Both the outgoing member (finishing its in-flight batch) and the incoming member (starting from the last committed offset) process the same records.
- **Detection:** duplicates cluster around deploy/scale-event timestamps; two different consumer instances (different group-generation/epoch) processed the same job_id; a Kafka `CommitFailedException` around the same time.
- **Fix:** commit the offset/ack strictly after the idempotent write commits, per-message rather than batched; use cooperative/incremental rebalancing and drain in-flight work in `onPartitionsRevoked` before releasing; if cross-topic exactly-once semantics are genuinely required, use the broker's native transactional processing (Kafka EOS transactions) instead of manual commit ordering.

### 7. Broker/coordinator split-brain or leader failover
A network blip causes two broker or coordinator replicas to each briefly believe they're the leader (degraded or misconfigured consensus), and each independently dispatches the same job — or a consumer reads a "still unclaimed" status from a stale, lagging replica.
- **Detection:** duplicates correlate with broker cluster events — leader-election logs, failover alerts, multi-leader warnings — not with any worker-side timing signal; a read-from-replica pattern in the architecture.
- **Fix:** quorum-based consensus (Raft/ZAB) for leader election, with epoch/fencing tokens on writes so a deposed leader's writes are rejected; route claim-checks to the current leader/primary only, never a replica.

### 8. Non-idempotent side effect despite genuinely single delivery
Delivery is fine — the broker shows exactly one dequeue — but something above the queue retries the handler after a partial failure (an application-level retry/circuit-breaker decorator wrapping the *entire* function, not just the failed sub-step), and the non-idempotent side effect has no idempotency guard of its own. This is a duplicate with a broker receive-count of 1: entirely self-inflicted, and invisible to every fix above.
- **Detection:** the clearest tell — broker/queue metadata shows one delivery, but downstream shows two effects; application logs show an internal retry trace, not a redelivery.
- **Fix:** never wrap a non-idempotent side effect in a blind "retry the whole function" decorator — retry only the failed sub-step, and checkpoint completed sub-steps so re-entry skips finished work.

### 9. Operational/configuration duplication (not a queue bug at all)
Two structurally independent paths feed or drain the same logical job: a producer publishing to two queues/topics during a migration (old and new systems both consuming to completion); a consumer-group identity that isn't stable — e.g., a deploy pipeline that generates a fresh Kafka `group.id` on every release instead of reusing one, so each deployment starts a brand-new group and reprocesses the topic from `earliest` alongside whatever prior deploys already consumed; or an orchestration layer sitting above the queue with its own "is this still running?" heartbeat/lock check, which fires early and submits a fresh job (new run_id) for what it wrongly believes is dead work, entirely bypassing the queue's own dedup because the queue sees it as a legitimately new message.
- **Detection:** two distinct job/message IDs, run_ids, or consumer-group IDs, same business payload, with full independent execution traces on each; steady-state duplication at a roughly constant rate uncorrelated with timeouts or crashes, but correlated with a recent deploy, migration cutover, or a too-aggressive orchestrator heartbeat timeout.
- **Fix:** single publish path (retire the dual-write once migration completes); pin consumer-group identity to something stable across deploys; tie orchestrator "still alive" checks to real execution heartbeats with a TTL wide enough to avoid false positives. In the interim, dedupe downstream on the business key regardless of how many independent paths produced the request.

## Most likely culprit

**Mechanism 1 (a visibility-timeout/lease race on a still-alive worker) compounding with mechanism 3 (a non-atomic idempotency check)** is the most likely cause in practice, by a wide margin.

- It requires no bug and no failure. Every other mechanism needs an actual crash, a network partition, a broker-specific edge case, a rebalance event, or a genuine configuration error — all comparatively rare and intermittent. A lease-timeout race just needs ordinary tail latency (a GC pause, a slow downstream call, a cold container, a load spike) to exceed a statically configured number, which happens continuously at any nontrivial scale — and its probability *rises* under load or dependency degradation, i.e., exactly when the system is already stressed and someone is watching closely enough to notice the duplicates.
- It's the failure mode every popular at-least-once queue's documentation explicitly disclaims — SQS, RabbitMQ, Celery/Redis, and Kafka with manual offset commits all say, in effect, "you must make your consumer idempotent," which is a tacit admission that this is the dominant residual case once their own delivery guarantees are exhausted.
- Teams very commonly implement the idempotency check as a plain `SELECT` followed by an `INSERT` rather than an atomic constraint. The moment two deliveries genuinely overlap because of mechanism 1, the TOCTOU gap in mechanism 3 is exactly what lets both through and turns a harmless redelivery into an actual duplicate side effect — a redelivery alone, caught by a correct atomic check, would have been invisible.
- Its detection signature — two executions of the same job_id with timestamps separated by roughly the configured visibility/lease timeout, the first worker's process still alive or finishing just after the second one starts — is the pattern that shows up overwhelmingly often in real incident postmortems, compared to the sharper but rarer signatures of clock skew, split-brain, or migration dual-writes.

**The fix that closes the largest share of this at once:** treat idempotency as an atomic, durable database constraint co-located with the side effect — a unique-constrained `processed_jobs` write in the same transaction as the work, gated by a single conditional statement rather than a preceding read — combined with heartbeat-based lease renewal (so a slow-but-alive worker never gets reassigned) and fencing tokens (so a genuinely dead worker's late write is rejected regardless of when it shows up). That combination directly closes mechanisms 1, 2, and 3, subsumes the clock-skew variant, and meaningfully reduces the blast radius of 5 and 6 as well — it's cheaper than trying to perfectly tune every timeout, rebalance, and leader-election edge case individually.

## Why "exactly-once" is a marketing term

You cannot make "do the work" and "durably record that the work was done" a single atomic step when those two acts span a network (worker ↔ broker ↔ downstream store). Over a lossy channel no protocol guarantees both ends agree the message was handled exactly once — this is the Two Generals problem. So every queue that advertises exactly-once actually implements **at-least-once delivery + de-duplication/idempotency = "effectively once."** (Consensus among brokers doesn't rescue this: it can agree on *delivery*, but it can't un-charge a card or un-send an email — the external, non-idempotent side effect is outside the transaction.)

That reframes the symptom precisely. Seeing a job "processed twice" requires **two things to coincide**: (1) the message was *delivered* more than once, or two messages represent one job, **and** (2) the idempotency layer failed to collapse them into one *effect*. A duplicate delivery hitting a correctly idempotent handler is a non-event. So the distinct mechanisms fall into three families: the delivery layer redelivered, the dedup layer had a gap, or duplication was injected above the queue.

---

## Family 1 — The delivery layer legitimately delivered more than once (at-least-once doing its job)

**1. Visibility-timeout / lease expiry vs. a slow-but-alive worker**
Processing outlasts the invisibility window (SQS visibility timeout, Kafka `max.poll.interval.ms`, a Redis lock TTL) because of a GC pause, cold start, or downstream latency spike. The broker assumes the worker died and redelivers to a second worker; the first also finishes.
- *Detection:* same job_id run by two different worker/host IDs, with the second pickup landing ≈ `first_pickup + visibility_timeout`; `ApproximateReceiveCount` (SQS) or delivery-count > 1 while the first process is still alive; correlates with p99 runtime approaching the timeout.
- *Fix:* heartbeat-renew the lease while working; set the base timeout above p99.9 runtime and alert as it's approached; add **fencing tokens** (a monotonically increasing token issued at lease acquisition) so a zombie worker's late write is rejected downstream. A clock-skew variant — a host with a slow NTP clock believing an expired lease is still valid — has the same fix (fencing tokens beat wall-clock lease comparison across hosts).

**2. Crash/partition between "side effect committed" and "ack sent"**
The worker completes the real work, then crashes, is OOM-killed, or loses the network before the ack (delete/offset-commit/status-flip) lands. No ack ⇒ redelivery.
- *Detection:* redelivery with evidence attempt #1 actually completed (a result/log from attempt #1 exists before the ack gap); duplicate outputs, same job_id, distinct trace IDs; gap ≈ the timeout interval.
- *Fix:* make completion durable *atomically with the work* — write a `processed_jobs(job_id UNIQUE)` row (or the result under a unique constraint) in the **same transaction** as the side effect, or use a **transactional outbox**; the ack becomes advisory and the DB constraint is the source of truth.

**3. Producer-side duplicate enqueue (ambiguous publish)**
The producer publishes, the broker accepts it, but the broker's ack back to the producer is lost. The producer retries and creates a *second, distinct* message for one logical job. This is duplicate *enqueue* masquerading as duplicate *processing*.
- *Detection:* two **different** message IDs, same business payload/key, enqueued within one retry-timeout window; same producer request ID in logs.
- *Fix:* an idempotent producer (Kafka producer-ID + sequence number; SQS FIFO `MessageDeduplicationId`) or a business-derived idempotency key enforced by a unique constraint at ingestion. Critically, the key must be generated **once at the call site and reused across retries**, not regenerated per attempt.

**4. Consumer-group rebalance / zombie consumer**
A rebalance (deploy, autoscale, spot eviction) reassigns a partition mid-flight. The old consumer finishes and commits after the new owner has already reprocessed from the last committed offset. A network partition or misconfigured consensus lets two broker/coordinator replicas each believe they're leader and dispatch the same job; or a claim-check reads a lagging replica that still shows the job unclaimed.

## Family 2 — Delivery was fine (or should have been caught) but the idempotency layer has a gap

**6. Non-atomic check-then-act (TOCTOU)**
Two overlapping deliveries both run `SELECT "processed?"` → false, both proceed, both write "processed." The check-then-act isn't atomic. Same bug in a DB-backed queue: `SELECT` eligible rows then a separate `UPDATE status='claimed'` lets two workers grab the same row.

Delivery is genuinely single, but the effect isn't guarded (blind `INSERT`, `counter += 1`, unconditional send); or dedup is keyed on the queue's internal message ID, which cannot catch the two-distinct-messages case from mechanism #3.

A retry/circuit-breaker decorator wraps the entire job function and catches an exception thrown *after* the real side effect succeeded (e.g., in a post-processing step), re-running everything.

- **Fix:** heartbeat-based lease renewal (the worker actively extends its lease while still working) instead of a static timeout; set the base timeout comfortably above p99.9 duration. Add **fencing tokens** — a monotonically increasing token issued at lease acquisition, checked by every downstream write — so even a genuine zombie's late write is rejected. Fencing tokens close the clock-skew variant entirely, since they don't depend on wall-clock comparison at all.

### 2. Side effect completes, then a crash/partition loses the ack
The worker finishes the real work, then crashes, gets OOM-killed, or loses network before the ack/offset-commit/delete-message lands. The broker's default assumption is "no ack = not done," so it redelivers. The same pattern shows up at batch granularity — a worker pulls N messages, finishes several, then crashes before the batch-level ack/offset-commit, so the whole batch (including already-completed items) is redelivered — and is most dangerous when the side effect is a call to an external, non-idempotent system (charging a card, sending an email): if the crash lands between the external call succeeding and your own bookkeeping recording that fact, redelivery doesn't just re-run harmless internal logic, it repeats the external call.
- **Detection:** duplicate side effects close together in time; the first worker's logs end right after "work done" with no matching "ack sent" entry; broker shows redelivery shortly after. For the external-call variant, the external system's own logs (payment processor, email provider) show two operations where your internal job log shows only one. For the batch variant, duplicates cluster at batch boundaries and correlate with a mid-batch crash/restart.
- **Fix:** make completion durable and atomic *with* the work — write to a `processed_jobs(job_id UNIQUE)` table (or a transactional-outbox pattern) in the same local transaction as the side effect, then ack; the ack becomes advisory, the DB constraint is the real source of truth. For external calls, also write a "pending intent" record *before* making the call (not just a completion record after), and pass a stable, business-derived idempotency key to the external API itself wherever it's supported (Stripe-style idempotency keys), keyed on your own business identifier rather than the queue's internal message ID — so even a gap in your own crash recovery still gets deduped by the receiver. For batches, commit/ack per completed item, not once for the whole batch.

### 3. Non-atomic check-then-act (TOCTOU) in the claim or the idempotency check
Either the queue is a DB table where a consumer does a `SELECT` for eligible rows followed by a separate `UPDATE status='claimed'` (two workers can both `SELECT` the same row before either updates it), or the idempotency check itself is a plain "has this run? → no → run it → mark it run" sequence that isn't atomic. Two workers can pass the "not yet processed" check before either has recorded that it's processing.
- **Detection:** two executions with near-identical timestamps (true concurrency, not a timeout-driven redelivery gap); logs show both workers reading `exists=false`/`unclaimed` before either wrote; reproducible under concurrent load.
- **Fix:** replace read-then-write with a single atomic conditional statement — `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (job_id) DO NOTHING`, a unique constraint, or `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` / `UPDATE ... WHERE status='pending' RETURNING *` for DB-backed queues. The atomic statement becomes the actual gate for whether work proceeds, not a check that precedes it.

### 4. Producer-side duplicate enqueue (publish-ack loss)
The producer publishes, the broker accepts it, but the ack back to the producer times out or is lost. The producer retries, believing the publish never landed, creating a second, distinct message/job ID for the same logical unit of work. Both copies are legitimately delivered exactly once each — the duplication happened at enqueue time, not at delivery time.
- **Detection:** two *different* message/job IDs, identical business payload or idempotency key, enqueued within one retry-timeout window of each other, often sharing a producer request ID in logs.
- **Fix:** a stable, business-derived idempotency key generated once at the call site — never regenerated on each retry attempt — enforced with a unique constraint at ingestion, plus transport-level idempotent producers where available (Kafka producer-ID + per-partition sequence number, SQS FIFO `MessageDeduplicationId`). Note that transport-level dedup only catches retries of the *same* send call; it does nothing if the application generates a fresh ID per attempt.

### 5. Dedup store is a cache with a TTL/window, not a durable record
Idempotency is implemented as a bounded structure — a Redis key with an expiry, SQS FIFO's 5-minute dedup interval, or Kafka idempotent-producer state tied to the producer's session/epoch (a producer restart gets a new producer ID, silently resetting the dedup window). A redelivery or retry arriving after that window closes, or after the store itself restarted/evicted under memory pressure, looks brand new.
- **Detection:** the duplicate arrives after a gap longer than the configured TTL/window; correlates with a queue backlog, downstream outage, delayed retry, or a producer/cache restart.
- **Fix:** back the check with a durable, effectively unbounded store — a completed-jobs table with a unique constraint on job_id — and treat any cache in front of it purely as a fast-path optimization, never the sole gate.

### 6. Consumer-group rebalance mid-processing (zombie member)
A rebalance — new pod deployed, autoscaler event, pod eviction — reassigns a partition to a different consumer while the original member hasn't committed its offset yet. Both the outgoing member (finishing its in-flight batch) and the incoming member (starting from the last committed offset) process the same records.
- **Detection:** duplicates cluster around deploy/scale-event timestamps; two different consumer instances (different group-generation/epoch) processed the same job_id; a Kafka `CommitFailedException` around the same time.
- **Fix:** commit the offset/ack strictly after the idempotent write commits, per-message rather than batched; use cooperative/incremental rebalancing and drain in-flight work in `onPartitionsRevoked` before releasing; if cross-topic exactly-once semantics are genuinely required, use the broker's native transactional processing (Kafka EOS transactions) instead of manual commit ordering.

### 7. Broker/coordinator split-brain or leader failover
A network blip causes two broker or coordinator replicas to each briefly believe they're the leader (degraded or misconfigured consensus), and each independently dispatches the same job — or a consumer reads a "still unclaimed" status from a stale, lagging replica.
- **Detection:** duplicates correlate with broker cluster events — leader-election logs, failover alerts, multi-leader warnings — not with any worker-side timing signal; a read-from-replica pattern in the architecture.
- **Fix:** quorum-based consensus (Raft/ZAB) for leader election, with epoch/fencing tokens on writes so a deposed leader's writes are rejected; route claim-checks to the current leader/primary only, never a replica.

### 8. Non-idempotent side effect despite genuinely single delivery
Delivery is fine — the broker shows exactly one dequeue — but something above the queue retries the handler after a partial failure (an application-level retry/circuit-breaker decorator wrapping the *entire* function, not just the failed sub-step), and the non-idempotent side effect has no idempotency guard of its own. This is a duplicate with a broker receive-count of 1: entirely self-inflicted, and invisible to every fix above.
- **Detection:** the clearest tell — broker/queue metadata shows one delivery, but downstream shows two effects; application logs show an internal retry trace, not a redelivery.
- **Fix:** never wrap a non-idempotent side effect in a blind "retry the whole function" decorator — retry only the failed sub-step, and checkpoint completed sub-steps so re-entry skips finished work.

### 9. Operational/configuration duplication (not a queue bug at all)
Two structurally independent paths feed or drain the same logical job: a producer publishing to two queues/topics during a migration (old and new systems both consuming to completion); a consumer-group identity that isn't stable — e.g., a deploy pipeline that generates a fresh Kafka `group.id` on every release instead of reusing one, so each deployment starts a brand-new group and reprocesses the topic from `earliest` alongside whatever prior deploys already consumed; or an orchestration layer sitting above the queue with its own "is this still running?" heartbeat/lock check, which fires early and submits a fresh job (new run_id) for what it wrongly believes is dead work, entirely bypassing the queue's own dedup because the queue sees it as a legitimately new message.
- **Detection:** two distinct job/message IDs, run_ids, or consumer-group IDs, same business payload, with full independent execution traces on each; steady-state duplication at a roughly constant rate uncorrelated with timeouts or crashes, but correlated with a recent deploy, migration cutover, or a too-aggressive orchestrator heartbeat timeout.
- **Fix:** single publish path (retire the dual-write once migration completes); pin consumer-group identity to something stable across deploys; tie orchestrator "still alive" checks to real execution heartbeats with a TTL wide enough to avoid false positives. In the interim, dedupe downstream on the business key regardless of how many independent paths produced the request.

## Most likely culprit

**Mechanism 1 (a visibility-timeout/lease race on a still-alive worker) compounding with mechanism 3 (a non-atomic idempotency check)** is the most likely cause in practice, by a wide margin.

- It requires no bug and no failure. Every other mechanism needs an actual crash, a network partition, a broker-specific edge case, a rebalance event, or a genuine configuration error — all comparatively rare and intermittent. A lease-timeout race just needs ordinary tail latency (a GC pause, a slow downstream call, a cold container, a load spike) to exceed a statically configured number, which happens continuously at any nontrivial scale — and its probability *rises* under load or dependency degradation, i.e., exactly when the system is already stressed and someone is watching closely enough to notice the duplicates.
- It's the failure mode every popular at-least-once queue's documentation explicitly disclaims — SQS, RabbitMQ, Celery/Redis, and Kafka with manual offset commits all say, in effect, "you must make your consumer idempotent," which is a tacit admission that this is the dominant residual case once their own delivery guarantees are exhausted.
- Teams very commonly implement the idempotency check as a plain `SELECT` followed by an `INSERT` rather than an atomic constraint. The moment two deliveries genuinely overlap because of mechanism 1, the TOCTOU gap in mechanism 3 is exactly what lets both through and turns a harmless redelivery into an actual duplicate side effect — a redelivery alone, caught by a correct atomic check, would have been invisible.
- Its detection signature — two executions of the same job_id with timestamps separated by roughly the configured visibility/lease timeout, the first worker's process still alive or finishing just after the second one starts — is the pattern that shows up overwhelmingly often in real incident postmortems, compared to the sharper but rarer signatures of clock skew, split-brain, or migration dual-writes.

**The fix that closes the largest share of this at once:** treat idempotency as an atomic, durable database constraint co-located with the side effect — a unique-constrained `processed_jobs` write in the same transaction as the work, gated by a single conditional statement rather than a preceding read — combined with heartbeat-based lease renewal (so a slow-but-alive worker never gets reassigned) and fencing tokens (so a genuinely dead worker's late write is rejected regardless of when it shows up). That combination directly closes mechanisms 1, 2, and 3, subsumes the clock-skew variant, and meaningfully reduces the blast radius of 5 and 6 as well — it's cheaper than trying to perfectly tune every timeout, rebalance, and leader-election edge case individually.

# Comparison-based sorting requires Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case

## The decision-tree model

A *comparison-based* sort is any algorithm whose only access to the input elements is through pairwise comparisons of the form "is $a_i < a_j$?" ($\le$, $=$, $>$ are equivalent for this purpose). It may branch on the outcomes and move elements around, but it may **not** inspect the internal representation of a key in any other way.

Fix the input size $n$. Any such algorithm can be modeled as a **binary decision tree**:

- Each **internal node** is one comparison $a_i : a_j$.
- Its two outgoing edges correspond to the two possible outcomes.
- Each **leaf** is a halting state where the algorithm commits to a specific permutation of the input as the sorted output.
- Running the algorithm on one input traces a single root-to-leaf path; the number of comparisons performed on that input equals the **depth of the leaf reached**.

Two structural facts drive the proof.

**Fact 1 — the tree has at least $n!$ leaves.**
Assume the $n$ elements are distinct; there are $n!$ possible input orderings. For the algorithm to be correct it must, for each of these $n!$ inputs, apply the (distinct) rearrangement that produces sorted order. Suppose two different input permutations $\pi \ne \sigma$ reached the *same* leaf. Then the algorithm applies the *same* output rearrangement to both — but the rearrangement that sorts $\pi$ does not sort $\sigma$ (they need different permutations to become ordered), so the algorithm is wrong on at least one. Contradiction. Hence distinct input permutations reach distinct leaves, so the number of leaves is $L \ge n!$.

**Fact 2 — worst-case comparisons = tree height.**
The worst-case comparison count is the length of the longest root-to-leaf path, i.e. the height $h$ of the tree.

## The counting argument

A binary tree of height $h$ has at most $2^h$ leaves (level 0 has 1 node; each level at most doubles the count). Combining with Fact 1:

$$
2^{h} \;\ge\; L \;\ge\; n! \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad h \;\ge\; \log_2(n!).
$$

Now lower-bound $\log_2(n!)$ with an elementary estimate (no full Stirling needed). The top half of the factors — $n, n-1, \dots, \lceil n/2\rceil+1$, at least $n/2$ of them — are each $\ge n/2$, and the rest are $\ge 1$, so

$$
n! \;\ge\; \left(\frac{n}{2}\right)^{n/2}.
$$

Taking base-2 logs:

$$
\log_2(n!) \;\ge\; \frac{n}{2}\log_2\frac{n}{2} \;=\; \frac{n}{2}\bigl(\log_2 n - 1\bigr) \;=\; \Omega(n\log n).
$$

(Equivalently, Stirling gives the exact leading term $\log_2(n!) = n\log_2 n - n\log_2 e + O(\log n) = \Theta(n\log n)$, with $\log_2 e \approx 1.4427$.)

Therefore

$$
\boxed{\,h \;\ge\; \log_2(n!) \;=\; \Omega(n\log n).\,}
$$

Since $h$ is exactly the worst-case number of comparisons, **every correct comparison-based sort must make $\Omega(n\log n)$ comparisons on some input of size $n$.** $\blacksquare$

The bound is **tight**: merge sort and heap sort achieve $O(n\log n)$, so comparison sorting is $\Theta(n\log n)$.

*The essential idea is information-theoretic.* Identifying which of the $n!$ permutations is the correct answer requires $\log_2(n!) \approx n\log_2 n$ bits of information, and a binary comparison yields at most **one bit** per query. The limit is imposed by the *model of computation*, not by any algorithm's cleverness. (The same argument even bounds the *average* case: a binary tree with $L$ leaves has average leaf depth $\ge \log_2 L$, so averaging over the $n!$ equally likely inputs also gives $\Omega(n\log n)$.)

---

# Why radix sort does not violate the bound

Radix sort is **not a comparison-based algorithm**, so the theorem simply does not apply to it. There is no contradiction because there is no hypothesis to contradict — the lower bound is a statement about one restricted model, not about sorting in general. Concretely, radix sort breaks *both* assumptions the proof rests on.

**1. It never performs a comparison.**
Radix sort never asks "is $a_i < a_j$?". At each pass it extracts one digit (or byte, or fixed-width chunk) of a key and uses that digit's *value directly as an index* to place the element into one of $b$ buckets — a stable counting sort per digit. This is direct addressing into the key's *representation*, an operation that has no encoding as a binary comparison outcome, so the decision-tree model does not describe it at all.

**2. Each primitive step carries more than one bit.**
A digit-and-bucket step has branching factor $b$ (the radix), resolving $\log_2 b$ bits of ordering information per step, versus at most $1$ bit for a comparison. The decision-tree constraint $2^h \ge L$ is replaced by the far more permissive $b^{\,h} \ge L$. Radix sort assumes structure the comparison model is explicitly forbidden to use: that keys are $d$-digit values in a known base whose digits can be indexed.

**Its complexity, and where the linear time comes from.**
LSD radix sort runs in

$$
O\bigl(d\,(n+b)\bigr),
$$

where $d$ is the number of digit-passes and $b$ is the radix. This is not $O(n\log n)$ *in $n$ alone* — it hides a dependence on the key width $d$ and the range of values. Two regimes make the picture precise:

- **Bounded / fixed-width keys.** For, say, 32-bit integers or fixed-length strings, $d$ is a constant independent of $n$. Choosing $b = \Theta(n)$ makes each pass $O(n)$, so the total is $O(dn) = O(n)$. This is **genuinely linear and genuinely beats every comparison sort** — it is not an illusion. There is no contradiction: radix sort exploits extra structure (indexable digits of bounded-width keys) that lies outside the comparison model, trading the generality of "keys are opaque, comparable-only objects" for "keys are fixed-radix numbers I can index into." The price is $O(b)$ auxiliary space for the bucket array.

- **Genuinely growing keys.** To have $n$ *distinct* integer keys you need at least $\log_b n$ digits, i.e. $\Omega(\log n)$ bits per key. If you then fix a *constant* radix $b$, you get $d = \Theta(\log_b n)$ and total time $\Theta(n\log n)$ — no better than comparison sort. The linear-time result depends on letting the radix grow ($b = \Theta(n)$) so that each operation swallows $\Theta(\log n)$ bits at once. And radix sort cannot sort truly opaque or unbounded-length keys in linear time at all.

**The unifying information-theoretic view.**
Sorting requires $\log_2(n!) \approx n\log_2 n$ bits to pin down the correct permutation. Radix sort respects this floor exactly — it does not cheat information theory, it changes the *currency* per operation:

- A comparison supplies $1$ bit, so a comparison sort needs $\ge n\log n$ operations.
- A digit inspection supplies $\log_2 b$ bits. Over $d = \log_b(\text{key range})$ passes across $n$ elements, radix extracts

$$
n \cdot d \cdot \log_2 b \;=\; n\log_2(\text{key range})
$$

bits total — which is $\ge n\log_2 n$ whenever the range is at least $n$ (necessary for distinctness), i.e. always enough to fully sort, and fully consistent with the bound.

**Bottom line.** The $\Omega(n\log n)$ result is a theorem about the *comparison model*, proved by a decision-tree/information-theoretic argument that charges one bit per binary comparison. Radix sort steps outside that model — it reads key digits by direct array indexing, an operation worth $\log_2 b$ bits rather than $1$ — and additionally assumes bounded-width keys. So it is out of the theorem's scope, not a counterexample to it. For suitable (fixed-width, bounded-range) keys it legitimately achieves $O(n)$ time; for keys whose width must grow with $n$ under a constant radix it collapses back to $\Theta(n\log n)$; and in every regime it stays above the $\log_2(n!)$ information floor that the comparison bound is merely one instance of.
