# Part 1: The Ω(n log n) lower bound for comparison-based sorting

**Definition.** A *comparison-based* sorting algorithm is one that gains all of its information about the input purely through pairwise queries of the form "is $a_i < a_j$?" (or $\le$, $=$, $>$) — it is never permitted to inspect a key's internal representation (its bits, digits, or value) in any other way. This restriction on the *model of computation* is exactly what the argument below bounds; it is not a bound on sorting in general.

**The decision-tree model.**
Fix an input size $n$, and assume (WLOG, as the standard and hardest case) that the $n$ elements are distinct. Any comparison-based algorithm running on inputs of this size can be represented as a binary decision tree:

- Each internal node represents one comparison between two elements.
- Its two outgoing edges correspond to the two possible outcomes of that comparison, and determine which comparison the algorithm makes next.
- Each leaf represents the algorithm halting and declaring one specific permutation of the input as the sorted output.
- Running the algorithm on a particular input traces exactly one root-to-leaf path, determined by the outcomes of the comparisons actually made; the number of comparisons performed on that input equals the depth of the leaf reached.

**Fact 1 — the tree must have at least $n!$ leaves.**
There are $n!$ distinct permutations of $n$ distinct elements, and for each one, feeding that permutation to the algorithm as input requires the algorithm to output that exact permutation, correctly rearranged. Suppose two *different* input permutations led to the *same* leaf. Since a leaf commits the algorithm to one fixed output ordering, the algorithm would produce identical output for two inputs that require different outputs — so it would be wrong on at least one of them, contradicting correctness. Hence distinct permutations must reach distinct leaves, so the tree has $L \ge n!$ leaves.

**Fact 2 — the worst-case comparison count equals the height of the tree.**
Every root-to-leaf path in this tree is realized by some actual input (the tree is built exactly from the $n!$ executions), so the worst-case number of comparisons over all inputs of size $n$ is precisely the length of the longest root-to-leaf path — the height $h$ of the tree.

**Counting argument.**
A binary tree of height $h$ has at most $2^h$ leaves (induction: a height-0 tree is a single leaf, $2^0=1$; a height-$h$ tree is a root with two height-$(h-1)$ subtrees, each with at most $2^{h-1}$ leaves, so at most $2\cdot 2^{h-1}=2^h$ total). Combined with Fact 1:

$$2^h \ge L \ge n! \quad\Longrightarrow\quad h \ge \log_2(n!).$$

**Bounding $\log_2(n!)$.**
The top half of the factors in $n! = n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots 1$ — namely $n, n-1, \dots,$ down to roughly $n/2$ — number about $n/2$ terms, each of which is at least $n/2$. So

$$n! \;\ge\; \left(\frac{n}{2}\right)^{n/2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \log_2(n!) \;\ge\; \frac{n}{2}\log_2\frac{n}{2} \;=\; \Omega(n\log n).$$

(Stirling's approximation gives the sharper $\log_2(n!) = n\log_2 n - n\log_2 e + O(\log n) = \Theta(n\log n)$, the same asymptotic class — and this class is unaffected by the base of the logarithm, since $\log_b n = \Theta(\log_2 n)$ for any constant $b$.)

**Conclusion.**

$$h \ge \log_2(n!) = \Omega(n\log n).$$

Since $h$ is exactly the worst-case number of comparisons, **every comparison-based sorting algorithm must perform $\Omega(n\log n)$ comparisons on some input of size $n$.** The bound is tight: merge sort and heap sort achieve $O(n\log n)$, so comparison-based sorting has complexity exactly $\Theta(n\log n)$. (The same leaf-counting argument, via the fact that a binary tree with $L$ leaves has *average* leaf depth $\ge \log_2 L$, extends this to an $\Omega(n\log n)$ bound on the *average-case* number of comparisons too, over a uniformly random input permutation — though the problem only requires the worst case.)

**Why this is really an information-theoretic bound.** Identifying the correct one of $n!$ possible orderings requires $\log_2(n!) \approx n\log_2 n$ bits of information. Each comparison is a yes/no question — it supplies at most **one bit**. So at least $\log_2(n!)=\Omega(n\log n)$ comparisons are necessary, independent of how cleverly they're chosen. This is a limit imposed by the *model* — one-bit binary queries over otherwise-opaque keys — not by any algorithm's lack of ingenuity. That framing is exactly what explains radix sort.

# Part 2: Why radix sort does not violate this bound

**Radix sort is simply not a comparison sort, so the theorem does not apply to it — there is no contradiction because the hypothesis was never satisfied.** But "it's a different model" deserves to be made precise, both structurally and quantitatively, so that it's clear radix sort isn't secretly getting something for nothing.

**1. Its primitive operation is not a comparison.**
Radix sort processes keys digit-by-digit (typically via a stable counting sort on each digit position, from least- to most-significant digit). At each pass it takes one digit of a key — a value in $\{0, \dots, b-1\}$ for radix $b$ — and uses that *value directly as an index* into one of $b$ buckets. This is direct/indexed addressing into the key's representation, not a pairwise "is $a_i<a_j$?" query. It never compares two elements to each other. Since the decision-tree argument only bounds algorithms whose every step is a binary comparison, it has literally nothing to say about an algorithm whose steps are $b$-way digit lookups.

**2. Quantifying the model gap: a digit-lookup step carries more than one bit.**
A comparison has 2 possible outcomes, so it yields at most $\log_2 2 = 1$ bit. A digit-extraction-and-bucket step has $b$ possible outcomes, so it yields up to $\log_2 b$ bits in a single primitive operation. That's the real source of the speed-up: radix sort isn't doing the *same kind* of work faster, it's doing *richer* elementary operations.

**3. Making sure there's no free lunch: radix sort's honest cost.**
Radix sort's running time is $\Theta(d(n+b))$, where $d$ is the number of digit passes and $b$ is the radix (each pass costs $O(n+b)$ for a counting sort). To represent $n$ distinct keys you need $b^d \ge n$, i.e. $d \ge \log_b n$. Two regimes matter:

- *Fixed-width keys* (e.g. 32-bit integers, fixed-length strings), where the key domain size is a constant independent of $n$: $d$ is genuinely $O(1)$ for any fixed base, giving $O(n)$ time trivially. This is a legitimately different, more constrained problem than "sort $n$ arbitrary comparable items" — it presupposes a bound on key size that doesn't grow with $n$.
- *Keys that need $\Theta(\log n)$ bits to be guaranteed distinct* — the fair, apples-to-apples case matching the $n!$-permutations argument. Here, with a **fixed constant base** $b$ (say $b=2$ or $b=256$), $d=\Theta(\log_b n)=\Theta(\log n)$, so the total cost is $\Theta(n\log n)$ — it does **not** beat the comparison bound, it matches it exactly. The only way to cut the number of passes to $O(1)$ is to let the base grow with $n$: choosing $b = n^{\varepsilon}$ for any fixed constant $\varepsilon>0$ gives $d = O(1/\varepsilon) = O(1)$ passes and hence $O(n)$ time — but it costs $\Theta(n^\varepsilon)$ auxiliary space for the counting/bucket array (concretely, for $n=10^6$: base $b=10$ needs $d=6$ passes and cost $\approx 6n$; base $b=\sqrt{n}=1000$ needs only $d=2$ passes, cost $\approx 2n$, using $O(\sqrt n)$ space; base $b=n$ needs $d=1$ pass, cost $\approx 2n$, using $O(n)$ space). Linear time is bought by spending extra space to enlarge the per-step "alphabet" — a genuine time–space trade unlocked only by leaving the comparison-only model, not a free improvement over it.

**4. Reconciling with the information-theoretic view.**
Over $d$ passes examining $n$ elements each at $\log_2 b$ bits per digit, radix sort extracts a total of $n\cdot d\cdot\log_2 b = n\log_2(b^d)$ bits — and since $b^d$ must be at least the number of distinct key values it needs to resolve, this is consistent with, not a violation of, the same information-theoretic accounting that produced the $\Omega(n\log n)$ bound: when the key domain genuinely scales as $\Theta(n)$ (as it must to guarantee $n$ distinct values), the total bits examined converge back to $\Theta(n\log n)$ unless extra space is spent to enlarge $b$.

**Bottom line.** The $\Omega(n\log n)$ result is a lower bound for a specific, restricted computational model — algorithms that may only extract order information via one-bit pairwise comparisons of otherwise-opaque keys. Radix sort operates outside that model: it requires structured, digit-decomposable keys and exploits direct addressing by digit value, an operation that supplies more than one bit of information per step. That is why it is not a counterexample — and when its cost is audited honestly in bits examined per key, rather than in raw operation count, it turns out to pay for its speed either with an assumption of bounded key width or with extra space, never with a free violation of the information-theoretic limit.

Suppose two different input permutations $\pi \ne \sigma$ reached the *same* leaf. Then the algorithm applies the *same* output rearrangement to both — but the rearrangement that sorts $\pi$ does not sort $\sigma$ (they need different permutations to become ordered), so the algorithm is wrong on at least one. Contradiction. Hence distinct input permutations reach distinct leaves, so the number of leaves is $L \ge n!$.

**Fact 2 — worst-case comparisons = tree height.**
The worst-case comparison count is the length of the longest root-to-leaf path, i.e. the height $h$ of the tree.

## The counting argument

A binary tree of height $h$ has at most $2^h$ leaves (level 0 has 1 node; each level at most doubles the count). Combining with Fact 1:

$$
2^{h} \;\ge\; L \;\ge\; n! \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad h \;\ge\; \log_2(n!).
$$

Now lower-bound $\log_2(n!)$ with an elementary estimate (no full Stirling needed). The top half of the factors — $n, n-1, \dots, \lceil n/2\rceil+1$, at least $n/2$ of them — are each $\ge n/2$, and the rest are $\ge 1$, so

$$
n! \;\ge\; \left(\frac{n}{2}\right)^{n/2}.
$$

Taking base-2 logs:

$$
\log_2(n!) \;\ge\; \frac{n}{2}\log_2\frac{n}{2} \;=\; \frac{n}{2}\bigl(\log_2 n - 1\bigr) \;=\; \Omega(n\log n).
$$

(Equivalently, Stirling gives the exact leading term $\log_2(n!) = n\log_2 n - n\log_2 e + O(\log n) = \Theta(n\log n)$, with $\log_2 e \approx 1.4427$.)

Therefore

$$
\boxed{\,h \;\ge\; \log_2(n!) \;=\; \Omega(n\log n).\,}
$$

Since $h$ is exactly the worst-case number of comparisons, **every correct comparison-based sort must make $\Omega(n\log n)$ comparisons on some input of size $n$.** $\blacksquare$

The bound is **tight**: merge sort and heap sort achieve $O(n\log n)$, so comparison sorting is $\Theta(n\log n)$.

*The essential idea is information-theoretic.* Identifying which of the $n!$ permutations is the correct answer requires $\log_2(n!) \approx n\log_2 n$ bits of information, and a binary comparison yields at most **one bit** per query. The limit is imposed by the *model of computation*, not by any algorithm's cleverness. (The same argument even bounds the *average* case: a binary tree with $L$ leaves has average leaf depth $\ge \log_2 L$, so averaging over the $n!$ equally likely inputs also gives $\Omega(n\log n)$.)

---

# Why radix sort does not violate the bound

Radix sort is **not a comparison-based algorithm**, so the theorem simply does not apply to it. There is no contradiction because there is no hypothesis to contradict — the lower bound is a statement about one restricted model, not about sorting in general. Concretely, radix sort breaks *both* assumptions the proof rests on.

**1. It never performs a comparison.**
Radix sort never asks "is $a_i < a_j$?". At each pass it extracts one digit (or byte, or fixed-width chunk) of a key and uses that digit's *value directly as an index* to place the element into one of $b$ buckets — a stable counting sort per digit. This is direct addressing into the key's *representation*, an operation that has no encoding as a binary comparison outcome, so the decision-tree model does not describe it at all.

**2. Each primitive step carries more than one bit.**
A digit-and-bucket step has branching factor $b$ (the radix), resolving $\log_2 b$ bits of ordering information per step, versus at most $1$ bit for a comparison. The decision-tree constraint $2^h \ge L$ is replaced by the far more permissive $b^{\,h} \ge L$. Radix sort assumes structure the comparison model is explicitly forbidden to use: that keys are $d$-digit values in a known base whose digits can be indexed.

**Its complexity, and where the linear time comes from.**
LSD radix sort runs in

$$
O\bigl(d\,(n+b)\bigr),
$$

where $d$ is the number of digit-passes and $b$ is the radix. This is not $O(n\log n)$ *in $n$ alone* — it hides a dependence on the key width $d$ and the range of values. Two regimes make the picture precise:

- **Bounded / fixed-width keys.** For, say, 32-bit integers or fixed-length strings, $d$ is a constant independent of $n$. Choosing $b = \Theta(n)$ makes each pass $O(n)$, so the total is $O(dn) = O(n)$. This is **genuinely linear and genuinely beats every comparison sort** — it is not an illusion. There is no contradiction: radix sort exploits extra structure (indexable digits of bounded-width keys) that lies outside the comparison model, trading the generality of "keys are opaque, comparable-only objects" for "keys are fixed-radix numbers I can index into." The price is $O(b)$ auxiliary space for the bucket array.

- **Genuinely growing keys.** To have $n$ *distinct* integer keys you need at least $\log_b n$ digits, i.e. $\Omega(\log n)$ bits per key. If you then fix a *constant* radix $b$, you get $d = \Theta(\log_b n)$ and total time $\Theta(n\log n)$ — no better than comparison sort. The linear-time result depends on letting the radix grow ($b = \Theta(n)$) so that each operation swallows $\Theta(\log n)$ bits at once. And radix sort cannot sort truly opaque or unbounded-length keys in linear time at all.

**The unifying information-theoretic view.**
Sorting requires $\log_2(n!) \approx n\log_2 n$ bits to pin down the correct permutation. Radix sort respects this floor exactly — it does not cheat information theory, it changes the *currency* per operation:

- A comparison supplies $1$ bit, so a comparison sort needs $\ge n\log n$ operations.
- A digit inspection supplies $\log_2 b$ bits. Over $d = \log_b(\text{key range})$ passes across $n$ elements, radix extracts

$$
n \cdot d \cdot \log_2 b \;=\; n\log_2(\text{key range})
$$

bits total — which is $\ge n\log_2 n$ whenever the range is at least $n$ (necessary for distinctness), i.e. always enough to fully sort, and fully consistent with the bound.

**Bottom line.** The $\Omega(n\log n)$ result is a theorem about the *comparison model*, proved by a decision-tree/information-theoretic argument that charges one bit per binary comparison. Radix sort steps outside that model — it reads key digits by direct array indexing, an operation worth $\log_2 b$ bits rather than $1$ — and additionally assumes bounded-width keys. So it is out of the theorem's scope, not a counterexample to it. For suitable (fixed-width, bounded-range) keys it legitimately achieves $O(n)$ time; for keys whose width must grow with $n$ under a constant radix it collapses back to $\Theta(n\log n)$; and in every regime it stays above the $\log_2(n!)$ information floor that the comparison bound is merely one instance of.

# Part 1: The Ω(n log n) lower bound for comparison-based sorting

**Definition.** A *comparison-based* sorting algorithm is one that gains all of its information about the input purely through pairwise queries of the form "is $a_i < a_j$?" (or $\le$, $=$, $>$) — it is never permitted to inspect a key's internal representation (its bits, digits, or value) in any other way. This restriction on the *model of computation* is exactly what the argument below bounds; it is not a bound on sorting in general.

**The decision-tree model.**
Fix an input size $n$, and assume (WLOG, as the standard and hardest case) that the $n$ elements are distinct. Any comparison-based algorithm running on inputs of this size can be represented as a binary decision tree:

- Each internal node represents one comparison between two elements.
- Its two outgoing edges correspond to the two possible outcomes of that comparison, and determine which comparison the algorithm makes next.
- Each leaf represents the algorithm halting and declaring one specific permutation of the input as the sorted output.
- Running the algorithm on a particular input traces exactly one root-to-leaf path, determined by the outcomes of the comparisons actually made; the number of comparisons performed on that input equals the depth of the leaf reached.

**Fact 1 — the tree must have at least $n!$ leaves.**
There are $n!$ distinct permutations of $n$ distinct elements, and for each one, feeding that permutation to the algorithm as input requires the algorithm to output that exact permutation, correctly rearranged. Suppose two *different* input permutations led to the *same* leaf. Since a leaf commits the algorithm to one fixed output ordering, the algorithm would produce identical output for two inputs that require different outputs — so it would be wrong on at least one of them, contradicting correctness. Hence distinct permutations must reach distinct leaves, so the tree has $L \ge n!$ leaves.

**Fact 2 — the worst-case comparison count equals the height of the tree.**
Every root-to-leaf path in this tree is realized by some actual input (the tree is built exactly from the $n!$ executions), so the worst-case number of comparisons over all inputs of size $n$ is precisely the length of the longest root-to-leaf path — the height $h$ of the tree.

**Counting argument.**
A binary tree of height $h$ has at most $2^h$ leaves (induction: a height-0 tree is a single leaf, $2^0=1$; a height-$h$ tree is a root with two height-$(h-1)$ subtrees, each with at most $2^{h-1}$ leaves, so at most $2\cdot 2^{h-1}=2^h$ total). Combined with Fact 1:

$$2^h \ge L \ge n! \quad\Longrightarrow\quad h \ge \log_2(n!).$$

**Bounding $\log_2(n!)$.**
The top half of the factors in $n! = n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots 1$ — namely $n, n-1, \dots,$ down to roughly $n/2$ — number about $n/2$ terms, each of which is at least $n/2$. So

$$n! \;\ge\; \left(\frac{n}{2}\right)^{n/2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \log_2(n!) \;\ge\; \frac{n}{2}\log_2\frac{n}{2} \;=\; \Omega(n\log n).$$

(Stirling's approximation gives the sharper $\log_2(n!) = n\log_2 n - n\log_2 e + O(\log n) = \Theta(n\log n)$, the same asymptotic class — and this class is unaffected by the base of the logarithm, since $\log_b n = \Theta(\log_2 n)$ for any constant $b$.)

**Conclusion.**

$$h \ge \log_2(n!) = \Omega(n\log n).$$

Since $h$ is exactly the worst-case number of comparisons, **every comparison-based sorting algorithm must perform $\Omega(n\log n)$ comparisons on some input of size $n$.** The bound is tight: merge sort and heap sort achieve $O(n\log n)$, so comparison-based sorting has complexity exactly $\Theta(n\log n)$. (The same leaf-counting argument, via the fact that a binary tree with $L$ leaves has *average* leaf depth $\ge \log_2 L$, extends this to an $\Omega(n\log n)$ bound on the *average-case* number of comparisons too, over a uniformly random input permutation — though the problem only requires the worst case.)

**Why this is really an information-theoretic bound.** Identifying the correct one of $n!$ possible orderings requires $\log_2(n!) \approx n\log_2 n$ bits of information. Each comparison is a yes/no question — it supplies at most **one bit**. So at least $\log_2(n!)=\Omega(n\log n)$ comparisons are necessary, independent of how cleverly they're chosen. This is a limit imposed by the *model* — one-bit binary queries over otherwise-opaque keys — not by any algorithm's lack of ingenuity. That framing is exactly what explains radix sort.

# Part 2: Why radix sort does not violate this bound

**Radix sort is simply not a comparison sort, so the theorem does not apply to it — there is no contradiction because the hypothesis was never satisfied.** But "it's a different model" deserves to be made precise, both structurally and quantitatively, so that it's clear radix sort isn't secretly getting something for nothing.

**1. Its primitive operation is not a comparison.**
Radix sort processes keys digit-by-digit (typically via a stable counting sort on each digit position, from least- to most-significant digit). At each pass it takes one digit of a key — a value in $\{0, \dots, b-1\}$ for radix $b$ — and uses that *value directly as an index* into one of $b$ buckets. This is direct/indexed addressing into the key's representation, not a pairwise "is $a_i<a_j$?" query. It never compares two elements to each other. Since the decision-tree argument only bounds algorithms whose every step is a binary comparison, it has literally nothing to say about an algorithm whose steps are $b$-way digit lookups.

**2. Quantifying the model gap: a digit-lookup step carries more than one bit.**
A comparison has 2 possible outcomes, so it yields at most $\log_2 2 = 1$ bit. A digit-extraction-and-bucket step has $b$ possible outcomes, so it yields up to $\log_2 b$ bits in a single primitive operation. That's the real source of the speed-up: radix sort isn't doing the *same kind* of work faster, it's doing *richer* elementary operations.

**3. Making sure there's no free lunch: radix sort's honest cost.**
Radix sort's running time is $\Theta(d(n+b))$, where $d$ is the number of digit passes and $b$ is the radix (each pass costs $O(n+b)$ for a counting sort). To represent $n$ distinct keys you need $b^d \ge n$, i.e. $d \ge \log_b n$. Two regimes matter:

- *Fixed-width keys* (e.g. 32-bit integers, fixed-length strings), where the key domain size is a constant independent of $n$: $d$ is genuinely $O(1)$ for any fixed base, giving $O(n)$ time trivially. This is a legitimately different, more constrained problem than "sort $n$ arbitrary comparable items" — it presupposes a bound on key size that doesn't grow with $n$.
- *Keys that need $\Theta(\log n)$ bits to be guaranteed distinct* — the fair, apples-to-apples case matching the $n!$-permutations argument. Here, with a **fixed constant base** $b$ (say $b=2$ or $b=256$), $d=\Theta(\log_b n)=\Theta(\log n)$, so the total cost is $\Theta(n\log n)$ — it does **not** beat the comparison bound, it matches it exactly. The only way to cut the number of passes to $O(1)$ is to let the base grow with $n$: choosing $b = n^{\varepsilon}$ for any fixed constant $\varepsilon>0$ gives $d = O(1/\varepsilon) = O(1)$ passes and hence $O(n)$ time — but it costs $\Theta(n^\varepsilon)$ auxiliary space for the counting/bucket array (concretely, for $n=10^6$: base $b=10$ needs $d=6$ passes and cost $\approx 6n$; base $b=\sqrt{n}=1000$ needs only $d=2$ passes, cost $\approx 2n$, using $O(\sqrt n)$ space; base $b=n$ needs $d=1$ pass, cost $\approx 2n$, using $O(n)$ space). Linear time is bought by spending extra space to enlarge the per-step "alphabet" — a genuine time–space trade unlocked only by leaving the comparison-only model, not a free improvement over it.

**4. Reconciling with the information-theoretic view.**
Over $d$ passes examining $n$ elements each at $\log_2 b$ bits per digit, radix sort extracts a total of $n\cdot d\cdot\log_2 b = n\log_2(b^d)$ bits — and since $b^d$ must be at least the number of distinct key values it needs to resolve, this is consistent with, not a violation of, the same information-theoretic accounting that produced the $\Omega(n\log n)$ bound: when the key domain genuinely scales as $\Theta(n)$ (as it must to guarantee $n$ distinct values), the total bits examined converge back to $\Theta(n\log n)$ unless extra space is spent to enlarge $b$.

**Bottom line.** The $\Omega(n\log n)$ result is a lower bound for a specific, restricted computational model — algorithms that may only extract order information via one-bit pairwise comparisons of otherwise-opaque keys. Radix sort operates outside that model: it requires structured, digit-decomposable keys and exploits direct addressing by digit value, an operation that supplies more than one bit of information per step. That is why it is not a counterexample — and when its cost is audited honestly in bits examined per key, rather than in raw operation count, it turns out to pay for its speed either with an assumption of bounded key width or with extra space, never with a free violation of the information-theoretic limit.
